**[Disney Explorers Lodge](https://klook.tpm.li/XKSd3yKW)**

**[Disney’s Hollywood Hotel](https://klook.tpm.li/mHxofW0S)**

## **Special Experiences**

![Hong Kong Disneyland Trip Costs ant man](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/photo_2025-07-16-14.19.51-400x533.jpeg.webp)

![Hong Kong Disneyland Trip Costs ant man](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/photo_2025-07-16-14.19.51-400x533.jpeg.webp)

There are some special experiences at the Hong Kong Disneyland resort that you can purchase as add-ons to your trip. I will go through some of them below but just know that, since these are special experiences, I won’t be including them in the sample budget I have laid out below. 

The Explorer’s Club Semi-Buffet lunch costs HK$298 or US$38.23 per adult and HK$198 or US$25.40 per child. If you want to do the Explorer’s Club Semi-Buffet Dinner which includes access to a special viewing area for Momentous, the price of that is going to be HK$558 or US$71.58 per adult and HK$382 or US$49 per child.

![](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/photo_2025-11-05-23.15.20-400x533.jpeg)

![](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/photo_2025-11-05-23.15.20-400x533.jpeg)

The Crystal Lotus Disney Dim Sum Lunch Set goes for HK$428 or US$54.90 per person. This is the dining experience where you can eat dim sum that looks like various Disney characters. If you’d like to participate in a dim sum making class in addition to the dim sum lunch, that will cost HK$1,388 or US$178.04 per person. 

If you’d like to participate in the Starlight Picnic at the Hong Kong Disneyland Hotel, you have two options. The first is to do the regular picnic, which includes reserved seating for a Disney movie under the stars, as well as some light refreshments. This happens from 7pm-9pm daily and costs HK$280 or US$35.92 for two guests. The second option is similar but takes place from 9:30pm-11pm, includes more food selections, and includes access to the reserved viewing for Momentous. You would basically watch Momentous then go to the Hong Kong Disneyland hotel afterwards for the rest of the experience. This costs HK$880 or US$112.88 for two guests. 

![Hong Kong Disneyland Trip Costs space mountain](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/photo_2025-11-03-15.23.46-600x450.jpeg)

![Hong Kong Disneyland Trip Costs space mountain](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/photo_2025-11-03-15.23.46-600x450.jpeg)

Finally, there are a bunch of in-room surprises that you can purchase as add-ons to your hotel stay. By purchasing one of these, cast members will decorate your room and will leave a bunch of free goodies in your room for you to enjoy. They come in a variety of different themes like Mickey and Friends and Frozen. There are also many options for you to choose from, including lite and premium versions. They go for as low as HK$599 or US$76.84 per room and as high as HK$1,149 or US$147.39 per room. 

## **Sample Hong Kong Disneyland Trip Costs + Budget**

![](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/photo_2025-11-05-23.15.40-400x533.jpeg)

![](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/photo_2025-11-05-23.15.40-400x533.jpeg)

So for this sample budget I’m going to assume that your party is a family of 4 with two kids aged 7 and 9. You’re staying at Disney’s Hollywood Hotel for two nights in mid-tier season. You also have 4 2-day park tickets for each of you, using the Non-stop 2 Day Fun Ticket offer in the Tier 2 class. You’re purchasing Early Park Entry Passes for one of your park days and Premier Access for the other. 

![Hong Kong Disneyland Trip Costs iron man](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/photo_2025-07-16-14.20.49-600x450.jpeg.webp)

![Hong Kong Disneyland Trip Costs iron man](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/photo_2025-07-16-14.20.49-600x450.jpeg.webp)

You’ll be dining at quick service locations for lunch and dinner and decide that on one day you will eat breakfast at the hotel, and on another you’ll get something to eat at the park. Your kids also eat the kids meals that are available. You buy one snack each per day and spend the recommended amount on merchandise per person. You also take a taxi to HKDL from the city and then take a taxi from HKDL to the airport. You did not purchase any special experiences. 

Also, I’m not including airfare as airfare will vary greatly depending on where you’re coming from. Just something to keep in mind. 

Here’s how the budget looks like:

|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Parent 1 | Parent 2 | Child 1 | Child 2 | Subtotal |
| Transport to Resort (from City; 2 taxis) |  |  |  |  | HK$550 |
| Transport Out of Resort (to Airport; 2 taxis) |  |  |  |  | HK$400 |
| Hotel (2 nights) |  |  |  |  | HK$2,940 |
| Park Tickets | HK$859 | HK$859 | HK$669 | HK$669 | HK$3,056 |
| Early Entry (Day 1) | HK$199 | HK$199 | HK$199 | HK$199 | HK$796 |
| Premier Access (Day 2; 8 attractions pass) | HK$429 | HK$429 | HK$429 | HK$429 | HK$1,716 |
| Dining (Breakfast at Hotel) | HK$298 | HK$298 | HK$208 | HK$208 | HK1,012 |
| Dining (Breakfast at Park) | HK$100 | HK$100 | HK$100 | HK$100 | HK$400 |
| Dining (2 Lunches at the Park; both Quick Service) | HK$320 | HK$320 | HK$240 | HK$240 | HK$1,120 |
| Dining (2 Dinners at the Park; Both Quick Service) | HK$320 | HK$320 | HK$240 | HK$240 | HK$1,120 |
| Snacks (assume $70 per snack. Each person gets 1 snack a day for 2 days) | HK$140 | HK$140 | HK$140 | HK$140 | HK$560 |
| Merch | HK$400 | HK$400 | HK$400 | HK$400 | HK$1,600 |
| TOTAL |  |  |  |  | HK$15,270 or US$1958.72 |

All in all, a 2 day trip for a family of 4 may cost HK$15,270. This Hong Kong Disneyland budget is a sample that was made for your reference. Of course, the actual budget will depend on your specific situations and preferences. Maybe you don’t want to buy Premier Access or Early Entry, or will only stay in the hotel for one night. You could opt not to eat breakfast at the hotel, and commit to lowering your spending on merchandise. What I’m trying to say is that just because it may seem expensive, doesn’t mean it has to be. You can still make the necessary adjustments so that you lower your Hong Kong Disneyland trip costs and budget. 

## **Hong Kong Disneyland Trip Costs and Budget Conclusion**

![Hong Kong Disneyland Trip Costs tarzan](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/photo_2025-07-16-14.13.16-400x533.jpeg.webp)

![Hong Kong Disneyland Trip Costs tarzan](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/photo_2025-07-16-14.13.16-400x533.jpeg.webp)

Hopefully this Hong Kong Disneyland Trip Costs and Budget was a helpful guide for you to estimate and plan for your Hong Kong Disneyland trip. I hope you have the best time ever! No matter what you decide to do budget-wise, I can guarantee that you’ll have an amazing time. If you have any questions, feel free to reach me on my socials.

**Like me on Facebook at [The Disney Specialist](https://www.facebook.com/TheDisneySpecialist)****, and follow me on** [**Instagram**](https://www.instagram.com/stephenthedisneygeek/) **/** [**Twitter**](https://twitter.com/StephenJFlores) **/** [**Tiktok!**](https://www.tiktok.com/@stephenthedisneygeek)

If you enjoyed this Hong Kong Disneyland Trip Costs and Budget guide, consider **subscribing to my newsletter**and bring the Disney parks to your inbox. Whether you’re planning your first trip or your hundredth, **my newsletter is your ultimate guide to the magic of the parks.**

results.title: Hong Kong Disneyland 2026 Guide: What It ACTUALLY Costs + Ride Rankings
results.content: S tier. I cooled down with the Donald Duck ice cream bar for 60 Hong Kong dollars, which tasted like lemon cheesecake. Love that. Surprisingly refreshing. There was time for Mystic Manor, one of Hong Kong Disney's most popular attractions. The theming is incredible, and there was even a small connection to Tokyo Disney sees Tower of Terror in the pre-show. It's a dark ride that's cute, but also somehow intense. I loved it, and honestly wish Haunted Mansion would get this kind of upgrade. By this point, it was somehow already time for lunch. I ate at Explorers Club restaurant where I had the semibuffet butter chicken biryani set for 200 Hong Kong dollars. The buffet has food from all over the world, Indian, Russian, Western. Really solid options. Highly recommended. After that, I rested my [...] between tea and ice cream flavors. As night fell, the vibes at Hong Kong Disneyland [music] were amazing. So, I headed back to the world of Frozen. First was Frozen Ever After, a cute and epic ride with a surprising drop at the end. Then wandering oakans, sliding sleighs, a short but fun family coaster, great for kids. I found that riding it at night was quite thrilling. Dinner was at Golden Crocus Inn, where I had the lamb shank set for 240 Hong Kong dollars, meatballs with mushroom cream sauce for 55 Hong Kong dollars, and the forest mushroom pasta for $198 Hong Kong dollars. Definitely on the pricey [music] side, but really good. And finally, I wrapped up the night with momentous Hong Kong Disneyland's nighttime spectacular drones, fireworks, and projection mapping across the castle [...] solid options. Highly recommended. After that, I rested my feet on the Hong Kong Disneyland Railroad. It's more of a scenic ride than the one in Tokyo, but still a nice break for my feet. Then I picked up a massive turkey leg. Juicy, huge, and honestly pretty satisfying. Followed by these banana coconut fritters for 55 Hong Kong dollars from the Tahitan Terrace in Adventureland, which is a new addition to Hong Kong Disneyland. Next up was one of the best Disney shows I've ever seen, Festival of the Lion King. This felt like a mini Broadway production. Incredible dancing, singing, and energy. Absolute must-sea show S tier. If you’re planning a visit to Hong Kong Disneyland — or you’re just curious about theme park travel costs in Asia — this is a full, honest budget breakdown mixed with ride rankings and personal experience.

### Transcript:
I'm on DJ tour right now in Asia and today I am trading nightclubs for castles. So I'm in Hong Kong Disneyland for the first time and I'm real curious how much does it really cost? So in this video I'm going to break down all the prices from the food to the hotels, rate all the best rides and at the end of the video I'm going to tell you whether I think Hong Kong Disneyland is better than Tokyo Disneyland. Let's go. Let's kick off this video with how much a ticket costs for Hong Kong Disneyland. On this day, I paid $669 Hong Kong dollars for a tier 1 ticket, which is the cheapest pricing tier. Just keep in mind during busier seasons, the price does go up. One thing I immediately loved when I arrived is that you can actually grab a Starbucks inside the park, something you don't get at Tokyo Disneyland. So, I started with a vanilla sweet cream cold brew for 58 Hong Kong dollars, followed by a red velvet muffin for 37 Hong Kong dollars. Now, it's time to hit the rides. In this video, I'm ranking all the rides and shows using a tier system. S tier is amazing, and anything D tier or below, not so great. I started in Fantasy Land with Dumbo the Flying Elephant. That classic Disney ride everyone has to do at least once. Solid nostalgia. B tier. Next up, Cinderella Carousel. It's a pretty standard carousel. Great for kids, but a little awkward for me to ride solo. Ctier. Then came Madhatter Teacups, which had me feeling dizzy almost immediately. Not for me at all, but I know some people love it. Dtier. Next up is The Mini Adventures of Winnie the Pooh. A cute ride and a bit psychedelic at times and not quite as good as the Tokyo's version of this ride, but still pretty fun. C tier. Time to eat. And trust me, you're going to notice I eat a lot in this video. I tried the Zootopia waffle for 90 Hong Kong dollars. Crispy on the outside, soft inside. Honestly, really good. Then I had this carrotshaped German Christmas bun for 78 Hong Kong dollars packed with meat. Super messy, but it was very tasty. And I'm going to pause the video for a second here. If this is your first time here, I'm a Tokyo based DJ and music producer touring Asia. And if you're into nightife, travel, and unique experiences around Japan and Asia, feel free to subscribe. I drop new videos every week. All right, back to Disneyland. Over in Tomorrowland, I started with Ant-Man and the Wasp Nano Battle. A fun interactive shooter with lots of humor, and it was basically a walk-on. I really enjoyed this one. A tier. Next was Iron Man Experience. Similar to Star Tours. A lot of motion, lots of jokes, and cool 3D visuals of Iron Man flying through Hong Kong. Could make some people motion sick. B tier. Then came Hypers Space Mountain. The Star Wars overlay of Space Mountain. I'm a sucker for anything Star Wars, so this was an easy S tier for me. Next stop, World of Frozen, where I grabbed an Olaf ice cream bar for $70 Hong Kong dollars. After that, I headed to Toy Story Land and rode Slinky Dog Spin. Technically a kids ride, but surprisingly fun. I also tried the viral lot waffle sandwich, which was a winner. Love this. From here, I checked out the Hong Kong version of the Jungle River Cruise, which is similar to the Tokyo Disney one, except for the ending has fire effects and splashing water. Gets pretty intense. I actually enjoyed it a lot. Then I tried the Island Delight Slush for 68 Hong Kong dollars. Basically, it's a Doe Whip style icy drink, and I loved it. After that came what might be the best ride in the entire park, Big Grizzly Mountain Runaway Minec Cars. This coaster is insane. From the backwards drop to the speed [music] and surprises, this ride was an absolute highlight. S tier. I cooled down with the Donald Duck ice cream bar for 60 Hong Kong dollars, which tasted like lemon cheesecake. Love that. Surprisingly refreshing. There was time for Mystic Manor, one of Hong Kong Disney's most popular attractions. The theming is incredible, and there was even a small connection to Tokyo Disney sees Tower of Terror in the pre-show. It's a dark ride that's cute, but also somehow intense. I loved it, and honestly wish Haunted Mansion would get this kind of upgrade. By this point, it was somehow already time for lunch. I ate at Explorers Club restaurant where I had the semibuffet butter chicken biryani set for 200 Hong Kong dollars. The buffet has food from all over the world, Indian, Russian, Western. Really solid options. Highly recommended. After that, I rested my feet on the Hong Kong Disneyland Railroad. It's more of a scenic ride than the one in Tokyo, but still a nice break for my feet. Then I picked up a massive turkey leg. Juicy, huge, and honestly pretty satisfying. Followed by these banana coconut fritters for 55 Hong Kong dollars from the Tahitan Terrace in Adventureland, which is a new addition to Hong Kong Disneyland. Next up was one of the best Disney shows I've ever seen, Festival of the Lion King. This felt like a mini Broadway production. Incredible dancing, singing, and energy. Absolute must-sea show S tier. Afterwards, I tried the milk tea flavored slush for 62 Hong Kong dollars, which had a surprisingly good balance between tea and ice cream flavors. As night fell, the vibes at Hong Kong Disneyland [music] were amazing. So, I headed back to the world of Frozen. First was Frozen Ever After, a cute and epic ride with a surprising drop at the end. Then wandering oakans, sliding sleighs, a short but fun family coaster, great for kids. I found that riding it at night was quite thrilling. Dinner was at Golden Crocus Inn, where I had the lamb shank set for 240 Hong Kong dollars, meatballs with mushroom cream sauce for 55 Hong Kong dollars, and the forest mushroom pasta for $198 Hong Kong dollars. Definitely on the pricey [music] side, but really good. And finally, I wrapped up the night with momentous Hong Kong Disneyland's nighttime spectacular drones, fireworks, and projection mapping across the castle and the main street of Hong Kong Disneyland. Absolutely epic. After the park, I checked into the Hong Kong Disneyland Hotel. Classic Disney luxury vibes, nice rooms, theme amenities, and marble bathrooms. [music] They also have a big breakfast buffet with Mickeyshaped Hong Kong bubble waffles for $358 Hong Kong dollars. While I stopped here to rest my feet, I opened up one of those Hong Kong Disneyland trash can blind boxes that everyone else was buying. Not sure which area this is from, but it's a unique souvenir. If you're looking for cheaper options, there's also Disney Explorers Lounge, which is jungle themed, which honestly looked amazing. I think I'll have to stay here next time. The other option is Disney's Hollywood Hotel, which is the budget option with a classic Hollywood vibe with Marvel interiors. That night, I headed to the Marvel Bar at that hotel called The Archavist. As soon as I walked in, I was blown away. Movie props everywhere, Infinity Stones on display, even the Infinity Gauntlet. I ordered the Quantum Burgers for the $180 Hong Kong dollars. And then I went a little crazy with the lineup of cocktails. First up was the Potent Stones. [music] I mean, just look at that presentation. Incredible. Followed by the Redwing Flight. Then the Captain America themed Super Soldier Serum cocktail. Then I tried the Marvelous Punch. [music] And lastly, the strongest of them all was the Mischief cocktail. [music] I was feeling proper toasty after all those drinks, and the staff were kind enough to gift me this spicy chocolate Thor hammer to top it off. Great service. So, that was my full day at Hong Kong Disneyland with prices, rides, and my rankings. A lot of the rides had weights under 30 minutes on a weekday, so it's easy to get a lot done in just one day. If I compare this to Tokyo Disneyland, not Disney Se because that park is just too iconic. I honestly think Hong Kong Disneyland wins for me. Better food, shorter lines, great Marvel theming, [music] amazing shows, and I love not having to constantly book rides and meals on my phone. If you can handle some of the higher food prices, this is an absolute must visit Disney Resort. Thanks so much for watching to the end of the video. I know this is a little bit different than my usual style videos. If you guys enjoyed the video, be sure to give it a like. If you got any questions, drop it in the comments and be sure to subscribe because every week I'm dropping brand new videos. So until next time, V is

| Date format | dd/mm/yyyy  yyyy年mm月dd日 | [...] Food in Hong Kong is primarily based on Cantonese cuisine, despite the territory's exposure to foreign influences and its residents' varied origins. Rice is the staple food, and is usually served plain with other dishes. Freshness of ingredients is emphasised. Poultry and seafood are commonly sold live at wet markets, and ingredients are used as quickly as possible when still fresh. There are up to five daily meals: breakfast, lunch, afternoon tea, dinner, and siu yeh. Dim sum, as part of yum cha (brunch), is a dining-out tradition with family and friends. Dishes include congee, cha siu bao, siu yuk, egg tarts, and mango pudding. Local versions of Western food are served at cha chaan teng (Hong Kong-style cafes). Common cha chaan teng menu items include macaroni in soup, deep-fried French [...] Traditional Chinese family values, including family honour, filial piety, and a preference for sons, are prevalent. Nuclear families are the most common households, although multi-generational and extended families are not unusual. Spiritual concepts such as feng shui are observed; large-scale construction projects often hire consultants to ensure proper building positioning and layout. The degree of its adherence to feng shui is believed to determine a business's success. Bagua mirrors are regularly used to deflect evil spirits, and buildings often lack floor numbers with a 4; the number has a similar sound to the word for "die" in Cantonese.

### Cuisine

**Hong Kong**[[e]](#cite_note-20) is a [special administrative region](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special_administrative_regions_of_China "Special administrative regions of China") of [China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/China "China"). Situated on China's southern coast just south of [Shenzhen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen"), it consists of [Hong Kong Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island"), [Kowloon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon "Kowloon"), and the [New Territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories"). With 7.5 million residents in a 1,114-square-kilometre (430 sq mi) territory, Hong Kong is the [fourth-most densely populated region](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population_density "List of countries and dependencies by population density") in the world.

Hong Kong was established as a [colony of the British Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong") after the [Qing dynasty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") ceded [Hong Kong Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island") in 1841–1842 as a result of losing the [First Opium War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War"). The colony expanded to the [Kowloon Peninsula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Peninsula "Kowloon Peninsula") in 1860 and was further extended when the United Kingdom obtained a [99-year lease](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/99-year_lease "99-year lease") of the [New Territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories") in 1898. Hong Kong was [occupied](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_Hong_Kong "Japanese occupation of Hong Kong") by [Japan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") from [1941](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Hong_Kong "Battle of Hong Kong") to [1945](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liberation_Day_(Hong_Kong) "Liberation Day (Hong Kong)") during [World War II](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The territory was [handed over from the United Kingdom to China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong") in 1997. Hong Kong maintains separate governing and economic systems from those of [mainland China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") under the principle of [one country, two systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/One_country,_two_systems "One country, two systems").[[f]](#cite_note-21)

Originally an area of farming and fishing villages,[[16]](#cite_note-CarrollEarlyHistory-22)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERen2010221-23) Hong Kong is now one of the world's most significant [financial centres](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre") and [commercial ports](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Commercial_port "Commercial port"). Hong Kong is the world's [third-ranked global financial centre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index") behind [New York City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [London](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/London "London"), the ninth-largest exporter, and the eighth-largest importer in the world. Its currency, the [Hong Kong dollar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_dollar "Hong Kong dollar"), is the [ninth-most traded currency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Template:Most_traded_currencies "Template:Most traded currencies") globally. Home to the [third-highest number of billionaires](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_cities_by_number_of_billionaires "List of cities by number of billionaires") of any city in the world, Hong Kong has the second largest number of [ultra high-net-worth individuals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ultra_high-net-worth_individual "Ultra high-net-worth individual").[[18]](#cite_note-24)[[19]](#cite_note-25)[[20]](#cite_note-26) The city has one of the highest [per capita incomes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita"), although severe [income inequality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_income_equality "List of countries by income equality") still exists among the population.

Hong Kong is [the region with the most skyscrapers in the world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_cities_with_the_most_skyscrapers "List of cities with the most skyscrapers"), even though [its housing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Housing_in_Hong_Kong "Housing in Hong Kong") is consistently in high demand. Hong Kong is the second most expensive residential property market in the world and is one of the [most expensive cities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_cities_for_expatriate_employees "List of most expensive cities for expatriate employees") in the world. Hong Kong is also one of [the most visited cities in the world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_cities_by_international_visitors "List of cities by international visitors").

Hong Kong is a [highly developed territory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") and has a [Human Development Index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") (HDI) of 0.955, [ranking eighth in the world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index"), and is currently the only place in Asia to be in the top ten. Hong Kong is categorized as an [Alpha+ city](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_city "Global city") by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network"). The city has the [highest life expectancy in the world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_life_expectancy "List of countries by life expectancy"), and has a [public transport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transport_in_Hong_Kong "Transport in Hong Kong") usage exceeding 90 per cent.
