The name of the territory, first [romanised](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") as "He-Ong-Kong" in 1780,[[23]](#cite_note-29) originally referred to a small inlet located between [Aberdeen Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ap_Lei_Chau "Ap Lei Chau") and the southern coast of Hong Kong Island. [Aberdeen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aberdeen,_Hong_Kong "Aberdeen, Hong Kong") was an initial point of contact between British sailors and local fishermen.[[24]](#cite_note-30) Although the source of the romanised name is unknown, it is generally believed to be an early phonetic rendering of the [Cantonese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") (or [Tanka Cantonese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tanka_people#Dialect "Tanka people")) phrase *hēung góng*. The name translates as "fragrant harbour" or "incense harbour".[[21]](#cite_note-HerbariumName-27)[[22]](#cite_note-CarrollHKName-28)[[25]](#cite_note-etym-31) "Fragrant" may refer to the sweet taste of the harbour's freshwater influx from the Pearl River or to the odour from incense factories lining the coast of northern [Kowloon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon "Kowloon"). The incense was stored near Aberdeen Harbour for export before [Victoria Harbour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victoria_Harbour "Victoria Harbour") was developed.[[25]](#cite_note-etym-31) [Sir John Davis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Francis_Davis "John Francis Davis") (the second colonial governor) offered an alternative origin; Davis said that the name derived from "Hoong-keang" ("red torrent"), reflecting the colour of soil over which a waterfall on the island flowed.[[26]](#cite_note-32)

The simplified name *Hong Kong* was frequently used by 1810.[[27]](#cite_note-33) The name was also commonly written as the single word *Hongkong* until 1926, when the government officially adopted the two-word name.[[28]](#cite_note-34) Some corporations founded during the early colonial era still keep this name, including [Hongkong Land](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hongkong_Land "Hongkong Land"), [Hongkong Electric Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hongkong_Electric_Company "Hongkong Electric Company"), [Hongkong and Shanghai Hotels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hongkong_and_Shanghai_Hotels "Hongkong and Shanghai Hotels"), and [the Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Hongkong_and_Shanghai_Banking_Corporation "The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation") (HSBC).[[29]](#cite_note-35)[[30]](#cite_note-HSBC-36)

## History

Main article: [History of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Hong_Kong "History of Hong Kong")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Hong Kong history](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Timeline_of_Hong_Kong_history "Timeline of Hong Kong history").

### Prehistory and Imperial China

The earliest known human traces in what is now called Hong Kong are dated by some to 35,000-39,000 years ago during the [Paleolithic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic") period. The claim is based on an archaeological investigation in [Wong Tei Tung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wong_Tei_Tung "Wong Tei Tung") in the [Sai Kung Peninsula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sai_Kung_Peninsula "Sai Kung Peninsula") in 2003. The archaeological works revealed [knapped](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Knapping "Knapping") [stone tools](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stone_tool "Stone tool") from deposits dated by optical [luminescence dating](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Luminescence_dating "Luminescence dating").[[31]](#cite_note-37)

During the Middle [Neolithic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") period, about 6,000 years ago, the region had been widely occupied by humans.[[32]](#cite_note-Meacham2-38) Neolithic to [Bronze Age](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") Hong Kong settlers were semi-coastal people. Early inhabitants are believed to have been [Austronesians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples") in the Middle Neolithic period, and later the [Yue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baiyue "Baiyue") people.[[32]](#cite_note-Meacham2-38) As hinted by the archaeological works in Sha Ha, Sai Kung, rice cultivation had been introduced since the [Late Neolithic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Late_Neolithic "Late Neolithic") period.[[33]](#cite_note-Li38-39) Bronze Age Hong Kong featured coarse pottery, hard pottery, quartz and stone jewelry, as well as small bronze implements.[[32]](#cite_note-Meacham2-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Sung_Wong_Toi.jpg/250px-Sung_Wong_Toi.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Sung_Wong_Toi.jpg)

[Sung Wong Toi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sung_Wong_Toi "Sung Wong Toi")

The [Qin dynasty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") incorporated the Hong Kong area into China for the first time in 214 BCE, after [conquering the indigenous Baiyue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qin_campaign_against_the_Yue_tribes "Qin campaign against the Yue tribes").[[34]](#cite_note-40) The region was consolidated under the [Nanyue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nanyue "Nanyue") kingdom (a predecessor state of [Vietnam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam")) after the Qin collapse,[[35]](#cite_note-KeatGinOoi-41) and it was recaptured by China after the [Han conquest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Han_conquest_of_Nanyue "Han conquest of Nanyue").[[36]](#cite_note-42) During the [Mongol conquest of China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mongol_conquest_of_the_Song_dynasty "Mongol conquest of the Song dynasty") in the 13th century, the [southern Song](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") court was briefly located in modern-day [Kowloon City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_City "Kowloon City") (the [Sung Wong Toi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sung_Wong_Toi "Sung Wong Toi") site) before its final defeat in the 1279 [Battle of Yamen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Yamen "Battle of Yamen") by the [Yuan dynasty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty").[[37]](#cite_note-Barber-43) By the end of the Yuan dynasty, seven large families had settled in the region and owned most of the land. Settlers from nearby provinces migrated to Kowloon throughout the [Ming dynasty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty").[[38]](#cite_note-44)

The earliest known European visitor was [Portuguese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal") explorer [Jorge Álvares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jorge_Álvares "Jorge Álvares"), who arrived in 1513.[[39]](#cite_note-45)[[40]](#cite_note-46) Portuguese merchants established a trading post called [Tamão](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamão "Tamão") in Hong Kong waters and began regular trade with southern China. Although the traders were expelled after [military clashes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Tunmen "Battle of Tunmen") in the 1520s,[[41]](#cite_note-47) Portuguese-Chinese trade relations were [re-established by 1549](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Luso-Chinese_agreement "Luso-Chinese agreement"). Portugal acquired a [permanent lease](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sino-Portuguese_Treaty_of_Peking "Sino-Portuguese Treaty of Peking") for [Macau](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Macau "Macau") in 1887.[[42]](#cite_note-48)

After the [Qing conquest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transition_from_Ming_to_Qing "Transition from Ming to Qing"), maritime trade was banned under the *[Haijin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haijin "Haijin")* policies. From 1661 to 1683, the population of most of the area that forms present-day Hong Kong was cleared under the [Great Clearance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Clearance "Great Clearance"), turning the region into a wasteland.[[43]](#cite_note-HK_story-49) The [Kangxi Emperor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kangxi_Emperor "Kangxi Emperor") lifted the maritime trade prohibition, allowing foreigners to enter Chinese ports in 1684.[[44]](#cite_note-50) Qing authorities established the [Canton System](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canton_System "Canton System") in 1757 to regulate trade more strictly, restricting non-Russian ships to the port of [Canton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou").[[45]](#cite_note-51) Although European demand for Chinese commodities like tea, silk, and porcelain was high, Chinese interest in European manufactured goods was insignificant, so that Chinese goods could only be bought with precious metals. To reduce the trade imbalance, the British sold large amounts of Indian [opium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opium "Opium") to China. Faced with a drug crisis, Qing officials pursued ever more aggressive actions to halt the opium trade.[[46]](#cite_note-52)

### British colony

Main article: [British Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Hong_Kong_1868.jpg/250px-Hong_Kong_1868.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_1868.jpg)

Hong Kong in 1868, photograph by [John Thomson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Thomson_(photographer) "John Thomson (photographer)")

In 1839, the [Daoguang Emperor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daoguang_Emperor "Daoguang Emperor") rejected proposals to legalise and tax [opium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opium "Opium") and ordered imperial commissioner [Lin Zexu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lin_Zexu "Lin Zexu") to eradicate the opium trade. The commissioner destroyed opium stockpiles and halted all foreign trade,[[47]](#cite_note-53) triggering a British military response and the [First Opium War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War"). The Qing surrendered early in the war and ceded Hong Kong Island in the [Convention of Chuenpi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convention_of_Chuenpi "Convention of Chuenpi"), signed by [Charles Elliot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Elliot "Charles Elliot") and [Qishan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qishan_(official) "Qishan (official)"). British forces began controlling Hong Kong shortly after the signing of the convention, on 26 January 1841.[[48]](#cite_note-54) However, both countries were dissatisfied and did not ratify the agreement.[[49]](#cite_note-55) After more than a year of further hostilities, Hong Kong Island was formally ceded to the [United Kingdom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") in the 1842 [Treaty of Nanking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Treaty_of_Nanking "Treaty of Nanking").[[50]](#cite_note-Courtauld-56)

Administrative infrastructure was quickly built by early 1842, but piracy, disease, and hostile Qing policies initially prevented the government from attracting commerce. Conditions on the island improved during the [Taiping Rebellion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taiping_Rebellion "Taiping Rebellion") in the 1850s, when many Chinese refugees, including wealthy merchants, fled mainland turbulence and settled in the colony.[[16]](#cite_note-CarrollEarlyHistory-22) Further tensions between the British and Qing over the opium trade escalated into the [Second Opium War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_Opium_War "Second Opium War"). The Qing were again defeated and were forced to give up [Kowloon Peninsula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Peninsula "Kowloon Peninsula") and [Stonecutters Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stonecutters_Island "Stonecutters Island") in the [Convention of Peking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convention_of_Peking "Convention of Peking").[[51]](#cite_note-SecondOpiumWar-57) By the end of this war, Hong Kong had evolved from a transient colonial outpost into a major [entrepôt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entrepôt "Entrepôt"). Rapid economic improvement during the 1850s attracted foreign investment, as potential stakeholders became more confident in Hong Kong's future.[[52]](#cite_note-58)

The colony was further expanded in 1898 when the United Kingdom obtained a 99-year lease of the New Territories.[[53]](#cite_note-NTLease-59) The [University of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Hong_Kong "University of Hong Kong") was established in 1911 as the territory's first institution of higher education.[[54]](#cite_note-60) [Kai Tak Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kai_Tak_Airport "Kai Tak Airport") began operation in 1924, and the colony avoided a prolonged economic downturn after the 1925–26 [Canton–Hong Kong strike](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canton–Hong_Kong_strike "Canton–Hong Kong strike").[[55]](#cite_note-KaiTak-61)[[56]](#cite_note-62) At the start of the [Second Sino-Japanese War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War") in 1937, Governor [Geoffry Northcote](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geoffry_Northcote "Geoffry Northcote") declared Hong Kong a neutral zone to safeguard its status as a free port.[[57]](#cite_note-63) The colonial government prepared for a possible attack, evacuating all British women and children in 1940.[[58]](#cite_note-64) The [Imperial Japanese Army](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Army "Imperial Japanese Army") [attacked Hong Kong on 8 December 1941](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Hong_Kong "Battle of Hong Kong"), the same morning as its [attack on Pearl Harbor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor").[[59]](#cite_note-65) Hong Kong was [occupied by Japan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_Hong_Kong "Japanese occupation of Hong Kong") for almost four years before the British resumed control on 30 August 1945.[[60]](#cite_note-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Flag_of_Hong_Kong_%281959%E2%80%931997%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_Hong_Kong_%281959%E2%80%931997%29.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Flag_of_Hong_Kong_1959.svg)

The [flag of British Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flag_of_Hong_Kong_(1871–1997) "Flag of Hong Kong (1871–1997)") from 1959 to 1997

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/578a_Hong_Kong_1971_%2851321892645%29.jpg/250px-578a_Hong_Kong_1971_%2851321892645%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:578a_Hong_Kong_1971_(51321892645).jpg)

[Peking Road](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peking_Road "Peking Road") in Tsim Sha Tsui in 1971

Its population rebounded quickly after the war, as skilled Chinese migrants fled from the [Chinese Civil War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") and more refugees crossed the border when the [Chinese Communist Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") took control of mainland China in 1949.[[61]](#cite_note-Wiltshire-67) Hong Kong became the first of the [Four Asian Tiger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Four_Asian_Tigers "Four Asian Tigers") economies to industrialise during the 1950s.[[62]](#cite_note-68) With a rapidly increasing population, the colonial government attempted reforms to improve infrastructure and public services. The [public-housing estate programme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_housing_in_Hong_Kong "Public housing in Hong Kong"), [Independent Commission Against Corruption](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Independent_Commission_Against_Corruption_(Hong_Kong) "Independent Commission Against Corruption (Hong Kong)"), and [Mass Transit Railway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MTR "MTR") were all established during the post-war decades to provide safer housing, integrity in the civil service, and more reliable transportation.[[63]](#cite_note-69)[[64]](#cite_note-70)

Nevertheless, widespread public discontent led to multiple protests from the 1950s to 1980s, including pro-[Republic of China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") and pro-[Chinese Communist Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") demonstrations. In the [1967 Hong Kong riots](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1967_Hong_Kong_riots "1967 Hong Kong riots"), pro-[PRC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/China "China") protestors clashed with the British colonial government. As many as 51 were killed, and 802 were injured in the violence, including dozens killed by the [Royal Hong Kong Police](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Royal_Hong_Kong_Police "Royal Hong Kong Police") via beatings and shootings.[[65]](#cite_note-71)

Although the territory's manufacturing competitiveness gradually declined due to rising labour and property costs, it transitioned to a service-based economy. By the early 1990s, Hong Kong had established itself as a global [financial centre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre") and shipping hub.[[66]](#cite_note-72)

### Chinese special administrative region

Further information: [Handover of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Avenue_of_Stars%2C_Hong_Kong_%282052819038%29.jpg/250px-Avenue_of_Stars%2C_Hong_Kong_%282052819038%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Avenue_of_Stars,_Hong_Kong_(2052819038).jpg)

[Victoria Harbour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victoria_Harbour "Victoria Harbour") and [Hong Kong Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island"), 2007

The colony faced an uncertain future as the end of the New Territories lease approached, and [Governor Murray MacLehose](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Murray_MacLehose,_Baron_MacLehose_of_Beoch "Murray MacLehose, Baron MacLehose of Beoch") raised the question of Hong Kong's status with [Deng Xiaoping](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") in 1979.[[67]](#cite_note-73) Diplomatic negotiations with China resulted in the 1984 [Sino-British Joint Declaration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sino-British_Joint_Declaration "Sino-British Joint Declaration"), in which the United Kingdom agreed to the handover of the colony in 1997, and China would guarantee Hong Kong's economic and political systems for 50 years after the handover.[[68]](#cite_note-Carroll181-74) The impending handover triggered a [wave of mass emigration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waves_of_mass_migrations_from_Hong_Kong "Waves of mass migrations from Hong Kong") as residents feared an erosion of civil rights, the rule of law, and quality of life.[[69]](#cite_note-75) Over half a million people left the territory during the peak migration period, from 1987 to 1996.[[70]](#cite_note-2002Pop-76) The Legislative Council became a [fully elected legislature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1995_Hong_Kong_legislative_election "1995 Hong Kong legislative election") for the first time in 1995. It extensively expanded its functions and organisations throughout the last years of the colonial rule.[[71]](#cite_note-77) The handover of Hong Kong to China was at midnight on 1 July 1997, after 156 years of British rule.[[72]](#cite_note-NYTHandover-78)

Immediately after the handover, Hong Kong was severely affected by several crises. The Hong Kong government was forced to use substantial [foreign exchange reserves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foreign_exchange_reserves "Foreign exchange reserves") to maintain the Hong Kong dollar's currency peg during the [1997 Asian financial crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis"),[[61]](#cite_note-Wiltshire-67) and the recovery from this was muted by an [H5N1 avian-flu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Influenza_A_virus_subtype_H5N1 "Influenza A virus subtype H5N1") outbreak[[73]](#cite_note-79) and a housing surplus.[[74]](#cite_note-80) This was followed by the [2003 SARS epidemic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2002–2004_SARS_outbreak "2002–2004 SARS outbreak"), during which the territory experienced its most serious economic downturn.[[75]](#cite_note-81)

Chinese communists portrayed the return of Hong Kong as a key moment in the PRC's rise to [great power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_power "Great power") status.[[76]](#cite_note-:172-82): 51

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Hong_Kong_anti-extradition_bill_protest_%2848108527758%29.jpg/250px-Hong_Kong_anti-extradition_bill_protest_%2848108527758%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_anti-extradition_bill_protest_(48108527758).jpg)

[Hong Kong protests](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2019–2020_Hong_Kong_protests "2019–2020 Hong Kong protests"), August 2019

Political debates after the handover have centred around the region's [democratic development](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Democratic_development_in_Hong_Kong "Democratic development in Hong Kong") and the [Chinese central government](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People's_Republic_of_China "State Council of the People's Republic of China")'s adherence to the "one country, two systems" principle. After reversal of the last colonial era Legislative Council [democratic reforms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1994_Hong_Kong_electoral_reform "1994 Hong Kong electoral reform") following the handover,[[77]](#cite_note-83) the regional government unsuccessfully attempted to enact [national security legislation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Security_(Legislative_Provisions)_Bill_2003 "National Security (Legislative Provisions) Bill 2003") pursuant to [Article 23 of the Basic Law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Basic_Law_Article_23 "Hong Kong Basic Law Article 23").[[78]](#cite_note-84) The central government decision to implement [nominee pre-screening](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2014_NPCSC_Decision_on_Hong_Kong "2014 NPCSC Decision on Hong Kong") before allowing [chief executive elections](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2014–2015_Hong_Kong_electoral_reform "2014–2015 Hong Kong electoral reform") triggered a series of [protests in 2014](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2014_Hong_Kong_protests "2014 Hong Kong protests") which became known as the Umbrella Revolution.[[79]](#cite_note-85) Discrepancies in the electoral registry and disqualification of elected legislators after the [2016 Legislative Council elections](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2016_Hong_Kong_legislative_election "2016 Hong Kong legislative election")[[80]](#cite_note-86)[[81]](#cite_note-87)[[82]](#cite_note-88) and enforcement of national law in the [West Kowloon high-speed railway station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_West_Kowloon_railway_station "Hong Kong West Kowloon railway station") raised further concerns about the region's autonomy.[[83]](#cite_note-89) In June 2019, [mass protests erupted](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2019–2020_Hong_Kong_protests "2019–2020 Hong Kong protests") in response to a [proposed extradition amendment bill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2019_Hong_Kong_extradition_bill "2019 Hong Kong extradition bill") permitting the extradition of fugitives to mainland China. The protests are the largest in Hong Kong's history,[[84]](#cite_note-EconomistRestoreCalm-90) with organisers claiming to have attracted more than three million Hong Kong residents.

#### Following the 2019 protests

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Hong_Kong_Tramways_103%28Z05%29_Shau_Kei_Wan_to_Happy_Valley_06-07-2020.jpg/250px-Hong_Kong_Tramways_103%28Z05%29_Shau_Kei_Wan_to_Happy_Valley_06-07-2020.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_Tramways_103(Z05)_Shau_Kei_Wan_to_Happy_Valley_06-07-2020.jpg)

A tram with slogans promoting the National Security Law printed on it

The Hong Kong regional government and the Chinese central government responded to the protests with many administrative measures to quell dissent. In June 2020, the Legislative Council passed the [National Anthem Ordinance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Anthem_Ordinance "National Anthem Ordinance"), which criminalised "insults to the national anthem of China".[[85]](#cite_note-91) The Chinese central government meanwhile enacted the [Hong Kong national security law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2020_Hong_Kong_national_security_law "2020 Hong Kong national security law") to help quell protests in the region.[[86]](#cite_note-cnn20200630-92) Nine months later, in March 2021, the Chinese central government introduced [amendments to Hong Kong's electoral system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2021_Hong_Kong_electoral_changes "2021 Hong Kong electoral changes"), which included the reduction of directly elected seats in the Legislative Council and the requirement that all candidates be vetted and approved by a Beijing-appointed [Candidate Eligibility Review Committee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Candidate_Eligibility_Review_Committee "Candidate Eligibility Review Committee").[[87]](#cite_note-france2420210311-93)

In July 2023, [Hong Kong's Legislative Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Hong_Kong "Legislative Council of Hong Kong") passed reforms slashing directly elected [District Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/District_Council_Eligibility_Review_Committee "District Council Eligibility Review Committee") seats and establishing a vetting committee for candidates.[[88]](#cite_note-dc2023_freepress01-94)[[89]](#cite_note-dc2023_gld01-95)[[90]](#cite_note-dc2023_gld02-96)

In terms of international connectedness, as of 2024, the city was one of eight worldwide that was classified as an "Alpha+" city by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network").[[91]](#cite_note-97)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Government of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Government_of_Hong_Kong "Government of Hong Kong"), [Politics of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Politics_of_Hong_Kong "Politics of Hong Kong"), [Elections in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elections_in_Hong_Kong "Elections in Hong Kong"), and [Separation of powers in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Separation_of_powers_in_Hong_Kong "Separation of powers in Hong Kong")

[![Large, round room with desks and a dais](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Legco_Complex_Chamber_20240420.jpg/250px-Legco_Complex_Chamber_20240420.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Legco_Complex_Chamber_20240420.jpg)

Since 2012, the legislature has met in the [Tamar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamar,_Hong_Kong "Tamar, Hong Kong") [Legislative Council Complex](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legislative_Council_Complex "Legislative Council Complex").

Hong Kong is a [special administrative region of China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special_administrative_regions_of_China "Special administrative regions of China"), with executive, legislative, and judicial powers [devolved](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") from the [national government](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Government_of_China "Government of China").[[92]](#cite_note-98) The Sino-British Joint Declaration provided for economic and administrative continuity through the handover,[[68]](#cite_note-Carroll181-74) resulting in an [executive-led](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") governing system largely inherited from the territory's history as a British colony.[[93]](#cite_note-BasicLaw15-99) Under these terms and the "one country, two systems" principle, the [Basic Law of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Basic_Law "Hong Kong Basic Law") is the regional constitution.[[94]](#cite_note-100) The regional government is composed of three branches:

* *[Executive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Government_of_Hong_Kong "Government of Hong Kong"):* As Hong Kong operates under an executive-led system, the [Chief Executive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chief_Executive_of_Hong_Kong "Chief Executive of Hong Kong") is responsible for enforcing regional law,[[93]](#cite_note-BasicLaw15-99) can force reconsideration of legislation,[[95]](#cite_note-101) and appoints [Executive Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Hong_Kong "Executive Council of Hong Kong") members and [principal officials](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principal_officials_of_Hong_Kong "Principal officials of Hong Kong").[[96]](#cite_note-102) Acting with the Executive Council, the [Chief Executive-in-Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/King-in-Council#The_Commonwealth "King-in-Council") can propose new bills, issue [subordinate legislation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Primary_and_secondary_legislation "Primary and secondary legislation"), and has authority to [dissolve](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament") the legislature.[[97]](#cite_note-103) In [states of emergency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/State_of_emergency "State of emergency") or public danger, the [Chief Executive-in-Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chief_Executive-in-Council "Chief Executive-in-Council") is further [empowered](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emergency_Regulations_Ordinance "Emergency Regulations Ordinance") to enact any regulation necessary to restore public order.[[98]](#cite_note-104)
* *[Legislature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Hong_Kong "Legislative Council of Hong Kong"):* The unicameral [Legislative Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Hong_Kong "Legislative Council of Hong Kong") enacts regional law, approves budgets, and has the power to [impeach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Impeachment "Impeachment") a sitting chief executive.[[99]](#cite_note-105)
* *[Judiciary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Judiciary_of_Hong_Kong "Judiciary of Hong Kong"):* The [Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Court_of_Final_Appeal "Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal") and lower courts interpret laws and overturn those inconsistent with the Basic Law.[[100]](#cite_note-Gittings150-106) The chief executive appoints judges on the advice of a recommendation commission.[[101]](#cite_note-107)

The chief executive is the [head of government](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") and serves for a maximum of two five-year terms, however in practice no chief executive has ever completed the maximum two five-year term limit.[[102]](#cite_note-108) The [State Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/State_Council_of_China "State Council of China") (led by the [Premier of China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Premier_of_China "Premier of China")) appoints the chief executive after nomination by the [Election Committee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Election_Committee "Election Committee"), which is composed of 1500 business, community, and government leaders.[[103]](#cite_note-109)[[104]](#cite_note-110)[[105]](#cite_note-111)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Hong_Kong_Government_House_08-09-2024%281%29.jpg/250px-Hong_Kong_Government_House_08-09-2024%281%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_Government_House_08-09-2024(1).jpg)

Hong Kong Government House

The Legislative Council has 90 members, each serving a four-year term. Twenty are directly elected from [geographical constituencies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geographical_constituency "Geographical constituency"), thirty represent [functional constituencies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Functional_constituency_(Hong_Kong) "Functional constituency (Hong Kong)") (FC), and forty are chosen by an [election committee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Election_Committee_(constituency) "Election Committee (constituency)") consisting of representatives appointed by the Chinese central government.[[106]](#cite_note-overhaul_HK-112) Thirty FC councillors are selected from limited electorates representing sectors of the economy or special interest groups.[[107]](#cite_note-LegCoVoting-113) Geographical constituency elected members are chosen by single non-transferable vote ([SNTV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_non-transferable_vote "Single non-transferable vote")), yielding two candidates per GC. The 30 limited electorate functional constituencies fill their seats using [first-past-the-post](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting") or [instant-runoff](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Instant-runoff_voting "Instant-runoff voting") voting.[[107]](#cite_note-LegCoVoting-113)

Twenty-two political parties had representatives elected to the Legislative Council in the [2016 election](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2016_Hong_Kong_legislative_election "2016 Hong Kong legislative election").[[108]](#cite_note-114) These parties have aligned themselves into three ideological groups: the [pro-Beijing camp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pro-Beijing_camp_(Hong_Kong) "Pro-Beijing camp (Hong Kong)") (the current government), the [pro-democracy camp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pro-democracy_camp_(Hong_Kong) "Pro-democracy camp (Hong Kong)"), and [localist groups](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Localism_in_Hong_Kong "Localism in Hong Kong").[[109]](#cite_note-115) However, by [2021](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2021_Hong_Kong_legislative_election "2021 Hong Kong legislative election"), the pro-democracy camp and the localist groups lost all representation in the Legislative Council as a result of the [2021 electoral changes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2021_Hong_Kong_electoral_changes "2021 Hong Kong electoral changes") imposed by the [National People's Congress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_People's_Congress "National People's Congress"),[[110]](#cite_note-116) and since [2025](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025_Hong_Kong_legislative_election "2025 Hong Kong legislative election") all 90 members of the Legislative Council have been from the pro-Beijing camp. The Chinese Communist Party does not have an official political presence in Hong Kong, and its members do not run in local elections.[[111]](#cite_note-117) Hong Kong is represented in the [National People's Congress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_People's_Congress "National People's Congress") by 36 deputies chosen through an electoral college and 203 delegates in the [National Committee of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Committee_of_the_Chinese_People's_Political_Consultative_Conference "National Committee of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference") appointed by the central government.[[8]](#cite_note-NationalReps-10)

[![The grey dome and front gable of a granite neo-classical building, with a skyscraper in the background against a clear blue sky](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Court_of_Final_Appeal_Building.jpg/250px-Court_of_Final_Appeal_Building.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Court_of_Final_Appeal_Building.jpg)

The [Court of Final Appeal Building](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Court_of_Final_Appeal_Building "Court of Final Appeal Building") formerly housed the [Supreme Court](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supreme_Court_(Hong_Kong) "Supreme Court (Hong Kong)") and the [Legislative Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Hong_Kong "Legislative Council of Hong Kong").

There are major differences between Hong Kong's legal system and the rest of China's.[[100]](#cite_note-Gittings150-106) Its judicial system is based on [common law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Common_law "Common law"), continuing the legal tradition established during British rule.[[112]](#cite_note-118) Local courts may refer to precedents set in [English law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/English_law "English law") and overseas jurisprudence.[[113]](#cite_note-119) However, [mainland criminal procedure law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Criminal_Procedure_Law_of_the_People's_Republic_of_China "Criminal Procedure Law of the People's Republic of China") applies to cases investigated by the [Office for Safeguarding National Security of the CPG in the HKSAR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Office_for_Safeguarding_National_Security "Office for Safeguarding National Security").[[114]](#cite_note-NPC_Observer-120) Interpretative and amending power over the Basic Law and jurisdiction over acts of state lie with the central authority, making regional courts ultimately subordinate to the mainland's [socialist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Socialist_law "Socialist law") [civil law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") system.[[115]](#cite_note-121) Decisions made by the [Standing Committee of the National People's Congress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standing_Committee_of_the_National_People's_Congress "Standing Committee of the National People's Congress") override any territorial judicial process.[[116]](#cite_note-HCAL185/2016-122) Furthermore, in circumstances where the Standing Committee declares a state of emergency in Hong Kong, the State Council may enforce national law in the region.[[117]](#cite_note-BasicLawA18-123)

The territory's jurisdictional independence is most apparent in its [immigration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visa_policy_of_Hong_Kong "Visa policy of Hong Kong") and [taxation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tax "Tax") policies. The [Immigration Department](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immigration_Department_(Hong_Kong) "Immigration Department (Hong Kong)") issues [passports for permanent residents](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Special_Administrative_Region_passport "Hong Kong Special Administrative Region passport") which differ from those of the mainland or Macau,[[118]](#cite_note-124) and the region maintains a [regulated border](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boundaries_of_Hong_Kong "Boundaries of Hong Kong") with the rest of the country. All travellers between Hong Kong, mainland China, and Macau must pass through border controls, regardless of nationality.[[119]](#cite_note-125) Mainland Chinese residents do not have [right of abode in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Right_of_abode_in_Hong_Kong "Right of abode in Hong Kong") and are subject to immigration controls.[[120]](#cite_note-126) Public finances are handled separately from the national government and taxes levied in Hong Kong do not fund the central authority.[[121]](#cite_note-127)[[122]](#cite_note-128)

The [Hong Kong Garrison](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Garrison "Hong Kong Garrison") of the [People's Liberation Army](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/People's_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") is responsible for the region's defence.[[123]](#cite_note-129) Although the [Chairman of the Central Military Commission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Chairman of the Central Military Commission (China)") is [supreme commander](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supreme_Military_Command_of_the_People's_Republic_of_China "Supreme Military Command of the People's Republic of China") of the armed forces,[[124]](#cite_note-130) the regional government may request assistance from the garrison.[[125]](#cite_note-131) Hong Kong residents are not required to perform military service, and current law has no provision for local enlistment. The garrison is composed entirely of non-Hongkongers.[[126]](#cite_note-132)

The central government and [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(China) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (China)") handle diplomatic matters, but Hong Kong retains the ability to maintain separate economic and cultural [relations with foreign nations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Hong_Kong "Foreign relations of Hong Kong").[[127]](#cite_note-133) The territory actively participates in the [World Trade Organization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), the [Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation "Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation") forum, the [International Olympic Committee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee"), and many [United Nations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") agencies.[[128]](#cite_note-134)[[129]](#cite_note-135)[[130]](#cite_note-136) It holds membership in other international bodies including the [World Health Organization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization").[[131]](#cite_note-:022-137): 201 The regional government maintains [trade offices](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Economic_and_Trade_Office "Hong Kong Economic and Trade Office") in [Greater China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greater_China "Greater China") and other nations.[[132]](#cite_note-138)

The imposition of the [Hong Kong national security law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2020_Hong_Kong_national_security_law "2020 Hong Kong national security law") by the [central government in Beijing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_People's_Congress_decision_on_Hong_Kong_national_security_legislation "National People's Congress decision on Hong Kong national security legislation") in June 2020 resulted in the suspension of bilateral extradition treaties by the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Finland, and Ireland.[[133]](#cite_note-rgmsus-139) The United States ended its preferential economic and trade treatment of Hong Kong in July 2020 because it was no longer able to distinguish Hong Kong as a separate entity from the People's Republic of China.[[133]](#cite_note-rgmsus-139)[[134]](#cite_note-bbcpref-140) In 2024, the [Safeguarding National Security Ordinance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Safeguarding_National_Security_Ordinance "Safeguarding National Security Ordinance") was passed by the Legislative Council to grant officials the power to prosecute crimes such as [treason](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Treason "Treason") and [insurrection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insurrection "Insurrection"). Critics state that this expansion will give more powers to crack down on opposition to the central government of China and the Hong Kong government as well as strike a lasting blow to the partial autonomy China had promised the city in the [Sino-British Joint Declaration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sino-British_Joint_Declaration "Sino-British Joint Declaration").[[135]](#cite_note-141)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Areas of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Areas_of_Hong_Kong "Areas of Hong Kong"), [Districts of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Districts_of_Hong_Kong "Districts of Hong Kong"), and [List of towns in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_towns_in_Hong_Kong "List of towns in Hong Kong")

Hong Kong's administrative divisions are divided into three levels: [Areas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Areas_of_Hong_Kong "Areas of Hong Kong") (區域), [districts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Districts_of_Hong_Kong "Districts of Hong Kong") (地區), and sub-districts (分區).[[136]](#cite_note-:12-142) Hong Kong is administratively divided into three areas: [Hong Kong Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island"), [Kowloon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon "Kowloon"), and the [New Territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories"). They are further divided into 18 districts. The area of Hong Kong Island has four districts, the area of Kowloon has five districts, and the area of the New Territories has nine districts.[[136]](#cite_note-:12-142)[[137]](#cite_note-:02-143) Each district is represented by a [district council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/District_councils_of_Hong_Kong "District councils of Hong Kong"). The district councils advise the government on local issues such as the provision of public facilities, the maintenance of community programmes, cultural promotion, and environmental policy.[[138]](#cite_note-district-144)[[139]](#cite_note-:4-145)[[89]](#cite_note-dc2023_gld01-95)

As of 2024,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Hong_Kong&action=edit) there are a total of 470 district council seats, 88 of which are directly elected.[[140]](#cite_note-146) In May 2023, the government proposed [reforms to the District Council electoral system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2023_Hong_Kong_electoral_changes "2023 Hong Kong electoral changes") which significantly cut the number of directly elected seats from 452 to 88, and total seats from 479 to 470. A requirement that district council candidates be vetted and approved by the [District Council Eligibility Review Committee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/District_Council_Eligibility_Review_Committee "District Council Eligibility Review Committee") was also implemented. The Legislative Council approved the reforms in July 2023.[[88]](#cite_note-dc2023_freepress01-94)[[89]](#cite_note-dc2023_gld01-95)[[90]](#cite_note-dc2023_gld02-96)

### Political reforms and sociopolitical issues

Main articles: [Democratic development in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Democratic_development_in_Hong_Kong "Democratic development in Hong Kong") and [Human rights in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human_rights_in_Hong_Kong "Human rights in Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/A_new_banner_on_the_lion_rock_-umbrellarevolution_-umbrellamovement_-occupyhk_-occupyhongkong_%2816134715622%29.jpg/250px-A_new_banner_on_the_lion_rock_-umbrellarevolution_-umbrellamovement_-occupyhk_-occupyhongkong_%2816134715622%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:A_new_banner_on_the_lion_rock_-umbrellarevolution_-umbrellamovement_-occupyhk_-occupyhongkong_(16134715622).jpg)

A yellow vertical protest banner which read "We demand real [universal suffrage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage")" was hung on [Lion Rock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lion_Rock "Lion Rock") during the [2014 Hong Kong protests](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2014_Hong_Kong_protests "2014 Hong Kong protests").

Hong Kong is governed by a [hybrid regime](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hybrid_regime "Hybrid regime") that is not fully [representative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") of the population. [Legislative Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Hong_Kong "Legislative Council of Hong Kong") members elected by functional constituencies composed of professional and special-interest groups are accountable to narrow corporate electorates rather than the general public. This electoral arrangement has ensured a [pro-Beijing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pro-Beijing_camp_(Hong_Kong) "Pro-Beijing camp (Hong Kong)") majority in the legislature since the handover. Similarly, the chief executive is selected by establishment politicians and corporate members of the Election Committee rather than directly elected.[[141]](#cite_note-hybridregime-147) Despite [universal suffrage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") being established as ultimate goals for the election of the chief executive and all members of the Legislative Council in [Articles 45](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Basic_Law_Article_45 "Hong Kong Basic Law Article 45") and 68 of the basic law,[[142]](#cite_note-148) the legislature is only partially directly elected, and the executive continues to be nominated by an unrepresentative body.[[141]](#cite_note-hybridregime-147) The government has been repeatedly petitioned to introduce direct elections for these positions, but has not introduced these direct elections as of 2026.[[143]](#cite_note-149)[[144]](#cite_note-150)

Ethnic minorities (except those of European ancestry) have marginal representation in government and often experience discrimination in housing, education, and employment.[[145]](#cite_note-151)[[146]](#cite_note-152) Employment vacancies and public service appointments frequently have language requirements that minority job seekers do not meet, and language education resources remain inadequate for Chinese learners.[[147]](#cite_note-153)[[148]](#cite_note-154) [Foreign domestic helpers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foreign_domestic_helpers_in_Hong_Kong "Foreign domestic helpers in Hong Kong"), mostly women from the Philippines and Indonesia, have little protection under regional law.[[149]](#cite_note-155) Although they live and work in Hong Kong, these workers are not treated as ordinary residents and do not have the right of abode in the territory.[[150]](#cite_note-156)

The Joint Declaration guarantees the [Basic Law of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basic_Law_of_Hong_Kong "Basic Law of Hong Kong") for 50 years after the handover.[[68]](#cite_note-Carroll181-74) According to legal scholar [Albert Chen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Chen_Hung-yee "Albert Chen Hung-yee"), the Basic law does not have an expiry date and is, unless amended, of continuing validity.[[151]](#cite_note-157) In response to [large-scale protests in 2019 and 2020](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2019–2020_Hong_Kong_protests "2019–2020 Hong Kong protests"), the [Standing Committee of the National People's Congress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standing_Committee_of_the_National_People's_Congress "Standing Committee of the National People's Congress") passed the controversial [Hong Kong national security law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2020_Hong_Kong_national_security_law "2020 Hong Kong national security law").[[152]](#cite_note-158) The law criminalises secession, subversion, terrorism and collusion with foreign elements and establishes the Office for Safeguarding National Security of the CPG in the HKSAR, an investigative office under Central People's Government authority immune from HKSAR jurisdiction. Some of the aforementioned acts were previously considered protected speech under Hong Kong law.[[114]](#cite_note-NPC_Observer-120)[[153]](#cite_note-159) The United Kingdom considers the law to be a serious violation of the Joint Declaration.[[154]](#cite_note-160) In October 2020, the Hong Kong Police arrested seven pro-[democracy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") politicians over tussles with pro-Beijing politicians in the Legislative Council in May. They were charged with contempt and with interfering with council members, while none of the pro-Beijing lawmakers were detained.[[155]](#cite_note-161) Annual commemorations of the [1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests_and_massacre "1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre") were also cancelled amidst fears of violating the national security law.[[156]](#cite_note-162) In March 2021, the Chinese central government [changed Hong Kong's electoral system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2021_Hong_Kong_electoral_changes "2021 Hong Kong electoral changes") and established the [Candidate Eligibility Review Committee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Candidate_Eligibility_Review_Committee "Candidate Eligibility Review Committee"), which would be tasked with screening and evaluating political candidates for their "patriotism", effectively crushing the remainder of the [pro-democracy camp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pro-democracy_camp_(Hong_Kong) "Pro-democracy camp (Hong Kong)").[[87]](#cite_note-france2420210311-93)

In February 2024, [Xia Baolong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xia_Baolong "Xia Baolong"), the head of the [Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_and_Macau_Affairs_Office "Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office"), said that the "[one country, two systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/One_country,_two_systems "One country, two systems")" principle would be kept permanently.[[157]](#cite_note-163)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geography_of_Hong_Kong "Geography of Hong Kong")

See also: [Land reclamation in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Land_reclamation_in_Hong_Kong "Land reclamation in Hong Kong") and [Environment of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environment_of_Hong_Kong "Environment of Hong Kong")

[![Satellite image showing areas of vegetation and conurbation.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Hong_Kong%2C_China.jpg/250px-Hong_Kong%2C_China.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong,_China.jpg)

Areas of urban development and vegetation are visible in this satellite image.

Hong Kong is situated on China's southern coast, 60 km (37 mi) east of Macau and on the eastern side of the [Pearl River estuary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pearl_River_Delta "Pearl River Delta"). The territory is surrounded by the [South China Sea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea") on all sides except the north, where it neighbors the [Guangdong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") city of [Shenzhen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") along the [Sham Chun River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sham_Chun_River "Sham Chun River"). Its standard area is approximately 1,110 km2 (430 sq mi),[[158]](#cite_note-:0-164) though this figure reaches approximately 2,750 km2 (1,060 sq mi)2[[158]](#cite_note-:0-164) when the open maritime area under its jurisdiction (rather than of [mainland China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China")) is included. The territory consists of [Hong Kong Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island"), the [Kowloon Peninsula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kowloon_Peninsula "Kowloon Peninsula"), the [New Territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories"), [Lantau Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lantau_Island "Lantau Island"), and over 200 other islands.

Of the standard area, 1,073 km2 (414 sq mi) is land and 35 km2 (14 sq mi) is inland water, such as reservoirs, rivers, and ponds.[[159]](#cite_note-cia-165) The territory's highest point is [Tai Mo Shan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tai_Mo_Shan "Tai Mo Shan"), reaching 957 metres (3,140 ft) above sea level.[[160]](#cite_note-166) Urban development is primarily concentrated on the Kowloon Peninsula, Hong Kong Island, and within various [new towns](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_towns_of_Hong_Kong "New towns of Hong Kong") throughout the New Territories.[[161]](#cite_note-167) Much of this is built on [reclaimed land](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Land_reclamation_in_Hong_Kong "Land reclamation in Hong Kong"); 70 km2 (27 sq mi) (representing 6% of the total land or about 25% of the territory's developed space) has been reclaimed from the sea.[[162]](#cite_note-reclamation-168)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Sai_Kung_Folded_Rock.JPG/330px-Sai_Kung_Folded_Rock.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Sai_Kung_Folded_Rock.JPG)

Twisted columnar jointing near the High Island Reservoir East Dam

Undeveloped terrain is hilly to mountainous, with minimal flat land, and consists mostly of grassland, woodland, shrubland, or farmland.[[163]](#cite_note-169)[[164]](#cite_note-170) About 40% of the remaining land area is [country parks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conservation_in_Hong_Kong "Conservation in Hong Kong") and nature reserves.[[165]](#cite_note-171) The territory has a diverse ecosystem; over 3,000 species of [vascular plants](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vascular_plant "Vascular plant") occur in the region (300 of which are native to Hong Kong), and thousands of insect, avian, and marine species.[[166]](#cite_note-172)[[167]](#cite_note-173) [Hong Kong UNESCO Global Geopark](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_UNESCO_Global_Geopark "Hong Kong UNESCO Global Geopark") was inaugurated on 3 November 2009. It is a single entity of land area over 150 km2 across parts of the eastern and northeastern [New Territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Territories "New Territories").[[168]](#cite_note-174) On 18 September 2011, [UNESCO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") listed the geopark as part of its [Global Geoparks Network](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_Geoparks_Network "Global Geoparks Network").

The Hong Kong UNESCO Global Geopark consists of two geological regions:[[169]](#cite_note-175)

* the [Sai Kung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sai_Kung_District "Sai Kung District") Volcanic Rock Region, with its widely distributed [tuff](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tuff "Tuff") [volcanic rocks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Volcanic_rocks "Volcanic rocks") displaying [prismatic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prism_(geometry) "Prism (geometry)") [columnar jointing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Columnar_jointing "Columnar jointing"), which are of international geological significance
* the Northeast New Territories Sedimentary Rock Region, which comprises [sedimentary rocks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sedimentary_rocks "Sedimentary rocks") formed in different geologic periods, showcasing the complete [geological history of Hong Kong.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geology_of_Hong_Kong "Geology of Hong Kong")

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Hong_Kong_Observatory.jpg/330px-Hong_Kong_Observatory.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_Observatory.jpg)

[Hong Kong Observatory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Observatory "Hong Kong Observatory") Headquarters

Hong Kong has a [humid subtropical climate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Köppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Cwa*), characteristic of southern China, closely bordering on a [tropical monsoon climate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tropical_monsoon_climate "Tropical monsoon climate"). Summers are long, hot, and humid, with occasional showers and thunderstorms and warm air from the southwest. The humid climate of Hong Kong intensifies summer heat. [Typhoons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Typhoon "Typhoon") occur most often then, sometimes resulting in floods or landslides. Also rarely occurring are [waterspouts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waterspout "Waterspout") and [tornadoes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tornado "Tornado"), which occurred at Hong Kong International Airport on 26 September 2020 and at Victoria Harbour on 28 September 2024.[[170]](#cite_note-176)[[171]](#cite_note-177) Winters are short, mild and usually sunny at the beginning, becoming cloudy towards February. Frequent cold fronts bring strong, cooling winds from the north and occasionally result in chilly weather. Autumn is the sunniest season, whilst spring is generally cloudy.[[172]](#cite_note-HKClimate-178) Snowfall has been extremely rare in Hong Kong; the last reported instance was on [Tai Mo Shan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tai_Mo_Shan "Tai Mo Shan") in 1975.[[173]](#cite_note-179) Hong Kong averages 1,709 hours of sunshine per year.[[174]](#cite_note-180) Historic temperature extremes at the [Hong Kong Observatory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Observatory "Hong Kong Observatory") are 36.6 °C (97.9 °F) on 22 August 2017 and 0.0 °C (32.0 °F) on 18 January 1893, record highest daily minimum temperature there was 30.1 °C (86.2 °F) on 18 August 1990 and the lowest daily maximum temperature there was 3.2 °C (37.8 °F) on 16 January 1893.[[175]](#cite_note-181)[[176]](#cite_note-182)[[177]](#cite_note-183) The highest and lowest recorded temperatures in all of Hong Kong are 39.0 °C (102 °F) at [Wetland Park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Wetland_Park "Hong Kong Wetland Park") on 22 August 2017,[[178]](#cite_note-184) and −6.0 °C (21.2 °F) at Tai Mo Shan [on 24 January 2016](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/January_2016_East_Asia_cold_wave "January 2016 East Asia cold wave").[[179]](#cite_note-185)

### Environment

Main article: [Air pollution in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Air_pollution_in_Hong_Kong "Air pollution in Hong Kong")

[Air pollution in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Air_pollution_in_Hong_Kong "Air pollution in Hong Kong") is considered a major problem. It became a concern soon after the start of the 2000s. According to the [Environmental Protection Department](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environmental_Protection_Department "Environmental Protection Department") (EPD), the major air [pollutants](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pollutant "Pollutant") in Hong Kong include [nitrogen oxides](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nitrogen_oxides "Nitrogen oxides") (NOx), [sulphur dioxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sulphur_dioxide "Sulphur dioxide") (SO2), respirable suspended [particulates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Particulates "Particulates") (RSP or PM10), fine suspended particulates (FSP or PM2.5), [volatile organic compounds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Volatile_organic_compound "Volatile organic compound") (VOC), [carbon monoxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide") (CO),[[183]](#cite_note-emissioninventory-190) [ozone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ozone "Ozone") and [lead](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lead "Lead").[[184]](#cite_note-AQOs-191)

### Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Architecture_of_Hong_Kong "Architecture of Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Yick_Cheong_Building_View_2015.jpg/250px-Yick_Cheong_Building_View_2015.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Yick_Cheong_Building_View_2015.jpg)

[Monster Building](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monster_Building "Monster Building"), a famous group of residential buildings in [Quarry Bay](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quarry_Bay "Quarry Bay")

Hong Kong has the world's [largest number of skyscrapers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_cities_with_the_most_skyscrapers "List of cities with the most skyscrapers"), with 554 towers taller than 150 metres (490 ft),[[185]](#cite_note-SkyscraperCenter-192) and the third-largest number of high-rise buildings in the world.[[186]](#cite_note-193) The lack of available space restricted development to high-density residential [tenements](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tenement "Tenement") and commercial complexes packed closely together on buildable land.[[187]](#cite_note-194) [Single-family detached homes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single-family_detached_home "Single-family detached home") are uncommon and generally only found in outlying areas.[[188]](#cite_note-195) The [International Commerce Centre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Commerce_Centre "International Commerce Centre") and [Two International Finance Centre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Finance_Centre_(Hong_Kong) "International Finance Centre (Hong Kong)") are the [tallest buildings in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Hong_Kong "List of tallest buildings in Hong Kong") and are among the tallest in the Asia-Pacific region.[[189]](#cite_note-196) Other distinctive buildings lining the Hong Kong Island skyline include the [HSBC Main Building](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HSBC_Building_(Hong_Kong) "HSBC Building (Hong Kong)"), the [anemometer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anemometer "Anemometer")-topped triangular [Central Plaza](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Plaza_(Hong_Kong) "Central Plaza (Hong Kong)"), the circular [Hopewell Centre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hopewell_Centre_(Hong_Kong) "Hopewell Centre (Hong Kong)"), and the sharp-edged [Bank of China Tower](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bank_of_China_Tower_(Hong_Kong) "Bank of China Tower (Hong Kong)").[[190]](#cite_note-FengShui-197)[[191]](#cite_note-198)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/HSBC_HK_building_at_night.jpg/250px-HSBC_HK_building_at_night.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:HSBC_HK_building_at_night.jpg)

[HSBC Building](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HSBC_Building_(Hong_Kong) "HSBC Building (Hong Kong)")

Demand for new construction has contributed to the frequent demolition of older buildings, freeing space for modern high-rises.[[192]](#cite_note-199) However, many examples of European and [Lingnan architecture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lingnan_architecture "Lingnan architecture") are still found throughout the territory. Older government buildings are examples of colonial architecture. The 1846 [Flagstaff House](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flagstaff_House,_Hong_Kong "Flagstaff House, Hong Kong"), the former residence of the [Commander of the British forces in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_Forces_Overseas_Hong_Kong "British Forces Overseas Hong Kong"), is the oldest Western-style building in Hong Kong.[[193]](#cite_note-200) Some buildings, such as the [Court of Final Appeal Building](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Court_of_Final_Appeal_Building "Court of Final Appeal Building") and the [Hong Kong Observatory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Observatory "Hong Kong Observatory") retain their original functions, and others have been [adapted and reused](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adaptive_reuse "Adaptive reuse"); the [Former Marine Police Headquarters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Former_Marine_Police_Headquarters "Former Marine Police Headquarters") was redeveloped into a commercial and retail complex,[[194]](#cite_note-TourismFacts-201) and [Béthanie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Béthanie_(Hong_Kong) "Béthanie (Hong Kong)") (built in 1875 as a [sanatorium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanatorium "Sanatorium")) houses the [Hong Kong Academy for Performing Arts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Academy_for_Performing_Arts "Hong Kong Academy for Performing Arts").[[195]](#cite_note-202) The [Tin Hau Temple](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tin_Hau_Temple,_Joss_House_Bay "Tin Hau Temple, Joss House Bay"), dedicated to the sea goddess [Mazu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mazu "Mazu") (originally built in 1012 and rebuilt in 1266), is the territory's oldest existing structure.[[196]](#cite_note-203) The [Ping Shan Heritage Trail](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ping_Shan_Heritage_Trail "Ping Shan Heritage Trail") has architectural examples from several imperial Chinese dynasties, including the [Tsui Sing Lau Pagoda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tsui_Sing_Lau_Pagoda "Tsui Sing Lau Pagoda") (Hong Kong's only remaining pagoda).[[197]](#cite_note-204)

*[Tong lau](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tong_lau "Tong lau")*, mixed-use tenement buildings constructed during the colonial era, blended southern Chinese architectural styles with European influences. These were especially prolific during the immediate post-war period, when many were rapidly built to house large numbers of Chinese migrants.[[198]](#cite_note-205) Examples include [Lui Seng Chun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lui_Seng_Chun "Lui Seng Chun"), the [Blue House](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blue_House_(Hong_Kong) "Blue House (Hong Kong)") in [Wan Chai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wan_Chai "Wan Chai"), and the [Shanghai Street shophouses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nos._600–626_Shanghai_Street "Nos. 600–626 Shanghai Street") in [Mong Kok](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mong_Kok "Mong Kok"). Mass-produced [public-housing estates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_housing_in_Hong_Kong "Public housing in Hong Kong"), built since the 1960s, are mainly constructed in [modernist style](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture").[[199]](#cite_note-206)

[![Skyline at night, with building lights reflected in water](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Hong_Kong_at_night.jpg/1280px-Hong_Kong_at_night.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_at_night.jpg "The Hong Kong Island skyline, viewed from the Victoria Harbour waterfront")

The Hong Kong Island skyline, viewed from the [Victoria Harbour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victoria_Harbour "Victoria Harbour") waterfront

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Kowloon_Panorama_by_Ryan_Cheng_2010.jpg/1920px-Kowloon_Panorama_by_Ryan_Cheng_2010.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Kowloon_Panorama_by_Ryan_Cheng_2010.jpg "City view of Kowloon, Hong Kong Island, and the Hong Kong skyline")

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Demographics_of_Hong_Kong "Demographics of Hong Kong") and [Hong Kong people](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_people "Hong Kong people")

Death rates (No. of Deaths per 100,000 Population) by leading causes of death, based on ICD 10th Revision.[[200]](#cite_note-207) Red: increased compared with 2001. Deaths from [dementia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dementia "Dementia") increased more than 5 times from 2001 to 2021.

| Cause of Death | 2001 | 2011 | 2021 |
| 1. Malignant neoplasms | 169.9 | 187.2 | 203.8 |
| 2. Pneumonia | 45.1 | 87.8 | **132.6** |
| 3. Diseases of heart | 70 | 89.6 | 89 |
| 4. Cerebrovascular | 46.6 | 47.2 | 42.2 |
| 5. External causes of  morbidity and mortality | 27.5 | 22.2 | 26.7 |
| 6. Nephritis, nephrotic  syndrome and nephrosis | 15.7 | 21.8 | 24 |
| 7. **Dementia** | 3.8 | 10.6 | **20.2** |
| 8. Septicaemia | 6.3 | 10.8 | 16.8 |
| 9. Chronic lower  respiratory diseases | 31.5 | 27.8 | 14.3 |
| 10. Diabetes mellitus | 10.1 | 6.5 | 7.4 |
| All other causes | 69.7 | 85 | 118.1 |
| All causes | 496 | 596.6 | 695.2 |

[![Population graph](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Bev%C3%B6lkerungspyramide_Hongkong_2016.png/250px-Bev%C3%B6lkerungspyramide_Hongkong_2016.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Bevölkerungspyramide_Hongkong_2016.png)

2016 [population pyramid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Population_pyramid "Population pyramid")

The [Census and Statistics Department](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Census_and_Statistics_Department_(Hong_Kong) "Census and Statistics Department (Hong Kong)") estimated Hong Kong's population at 7,413,070 in 2021. The overwhelming majority (91.6%) is [Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people"),[[6]](#cite_note-demographics-8) most of whom are [Taishanese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sze_Yap_people_in_Hong_Kong "Sze Yap people in Hong Kong"), [Teochew](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Teochew_people "Teochew people"), [Hakka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hakka_people "Hakka people"), and other [Cantonese peoples](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese_people "Cantonese people").[[201]](#cite_note-208)[[202]](#cite_note-209)[[203]](#cite_note-210) The remaining 8.4% are non-ethnic Chinese minorities, primarily [Filipinos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Filipinos_in_Hong_Kong "Filipinos in Hong Kong"), [Indonesians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesians_in_Hong_Kong "Indonesians in Hong Kong"), and [South Asians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Asians_in_Hong_Kong "South Asians in Hong Kong").[[6]](#cite_note-demographics-8)[[204]](#cite_note-211) However, most Filipinos and Indonesians in Hong Kong are short-term workers. According to a 2021 thematic report by the Hong Kong government, after excluding foreign domestic helpers, the real number of non-Chinese ethnic minorities in the city was 301,344, or 4% of Hong Kong's population.[[205]](#cite_note-212) About half the population have [some form of British nationality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law"), a legacy of colonial rule; 3.4 million residents have [British National (Overseas)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_National_(Overseas) "British National (Overseas)") status, and 260,000 British citizens live in the territory.[[206]](#cite_note-213) The vast majority also hold [Chinese nationality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_nationality_law "Chinese nationality law"), automatically granted to all ethnic Chinese residents at the handover.[[207]](#cite_note-214) Headline [population density](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Population_density "Population density") exceeds 7,060 people/km2 It is the [fourth-highest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population_density "List of countries and dependencies by population density") in the world.[[208]](#cite_note-215)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Sik_Sik_Yuen_Wong_Tai_Sin_Temple_%281944336752%29.jpg/250px-Sik_Sik_Yuen_Wong_Tai_Sin_Temple_%281944336752%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Sik_Sik_Yuen_Wong_Tai_Sin_Temple_(1944336752).jpg)

[Wong Tai Sin Temple](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wong_Tai_Sin_Temple_(Hong_Kong) "Wong Tai Sin Temple (Hong Kong)") is dedicated to the [Taoist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") deity [Wong Tai Sin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wong_Tai_Sin "Wong Tai Sin").

Among the religious population, the traditional "[three teachings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Three_teachings "Three teachings")" of China, [Buddhism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism"), [Confucianism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), and [Taoism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), have the most adherents (20%), followed by [Christianity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (12%) and [Islam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islam_in_Hong_Kong "Islam in Hong Kong") (4%).[[209]](#cite_note-religionFacts-216) Followers of other religions, including [Sikhism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), [Hinduism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), and [Judaism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), generally originate from regions where their religion predominates.[[209]](#cite_note-religionFacts-216)

Life expectancy in Hong Kong was 82.8 years for males and 88.4 years for females at birth in 2024.[[210]](#cite_note-217) The birth rate in 2023 was 0.751 per woman of child-bearing age.[[211]](#cite_note-218) [Cancer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cancer "Cancer"), [pneumonia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pneumonia "Pneumonia"), [heart disease](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease"), [cerebrovascular disease](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cerebrovascular_disease "Cerebrovascular disease"), and accidents are the territory's five leading causes of death.[[212]](#cite_note-2017HealthFacts-219) The [universal public healthcare system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") is funded by general-tax revenue, and treatment is highly subsidised; on average, 95% of healthcare costs are covered by the government.[[213]](#cite_note-220)

The city has a severe amount of income inequality,[[214]](#cite_note-221) which has risen since the handover, as the region's ageing population has gradually added to the number of nonworking people.[[215]](#cite_note-222) Although median household income steadily increased during the decade to 2021, the wage gap remained high;[[216]](#cite_note-IncomeDistribution-223) the 90th percentile of earners receive 41% of all income.[[216]](#cite_note-IncomeDistribution-223) The city has the most billionaires per capita, with one billionaire per 109,657 people,[[217]](#cite_note-224) as well as the [second-highest number of billionaires](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_cities_by_number_of_billionaires "List of cities by number of billionaires") of any city in the world,[[218]](#cite_note-225) the highest number of billionaires of any city in Asia, and the second largest concentration of [ultra high-net-worth individuals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ultra_high-net-worth_individual "Ultra high-net-worth individual") of any city in the world.[[219]](#cite_note-226)[[220]](#cite_note-227) Despite government efforts to reduce the growing disparity,[[221]](#cite_note-228) median income for the top 10% of earners is 57 times that of the bottom 10%.[[222]](#cite_note-229)[[223]](#cite_note-230)

## Economy

Main articles: [Economy of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economy_of_Hong_Kong "Economy of Hong Kong") and [Tourism in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tourism_in_Hong_Kong "Tourism in Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Kwai_Tsing_Container_Terminals.jpg/250px-Kwai_Tsing_Container_Terminals.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Kwai_Tsing_Container_Terminals.jpg)

[Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Port_of_Hong_Kong "Port of Hong Kong") is one of the world's [busiest container ports](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_busiest_container_ports "List of busiest container ports").

Along with [Singapore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [South Korea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), and [Taiwan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), Hong Kong is one of the [Four Asian Tigers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Four_Asian_Tigers "Four Asian Tigers").

One of the world's most significant financial centres and commercial ports,[[224]](#cite_note-231) Hong Kong has a [market economy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") focused on [services](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Service_economy "Service economy"), characterised by [low taxation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taxation_in_Hong_Kong "Taxation in Hong Kong"), minimal government market intervention, and an established international financial market.[[225]](#cite_note-232) It is the world's [37th-largest economy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)"), with a [nominal GDP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") of approximately US$446 billion.[[13]](#cite_note-IMFWEO.HK-15) Hong Kong is highly developed, and ranks fourth on the [UN Human Development Index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index").[[159]](#cite_note-cia-165) The [Hong Kong Stock Exchange](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Stock_Exchange "Hong Kong Stock Exchange") is the [fifth-largest in the world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_stock_exchanges "List of stock exchanges"), with a market capitalisation of HK$48.2 trillion (US$6.17 trillion) as of December 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Hong_Kong&action=edit).[[226]](#cite_note-233) Hong Kong was ranked as the 15th most innovative territory in the 2025 [Global Innovation Index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index"),[[227]](#cite_note-234)[[228]](#cite_note-235) and 3rd in the [Global Financial Centres Index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index").[[229]](#cite_note-GFCI-236) The city is sometimes referred to as "Silicon Harbour" in the 1990s, a nickname derived from [Silicon Valley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") in [California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California "California").[[230]](#cite_note-Tech_Monitor_1999-237)
