Hong Kong is the ninth largest trading entity in [exports](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports "List of countries by exports") and eighth largest in [imports](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_imports "List of countries by imports") (2021),[[231]](#cite_note-CIAExports-238)[[232]](#cite_note-CIAImports-239) trading more goods in value than its gross domestic product.[[231]](#cite_note-CIAExports-238)[[232]](#cite_note-CIAImports-239) Over half of its cargo throughput consists of [transshipments](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transshipment "Transshipment") (goods travelling through Hong Kong). Products from mainland China account for about 40% of that traffic.[[233]](#cite_note-LegcoEconomy-240) The city's location allowed it to establish a transportation and logistics infrastructure, which includes the world's seventh-busiest container port[[234]](#cite_note-241) and the busiest airport for international cargo.[[235]](#cite_note-242) The territory's largest export markets are mainland China and the United States.[[159]](#cite_note-cia-165) Hong Kong is a key part of the [21st Century Maritime Silk Road](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/21st_Century_Maritime_Silk_Road "21st Century Maritime Silk Road").[[236]](#cite_note-243) It has little arable land and few natural resources, importing most of its food and raw materials. More than 90% of Hong Kong's food is imported, including nearly all of its meat and rice.[[237]](#cite_note-244) Agricultural activity is 0.1% of GDP and consists of growing premium food and flower varieties.[[238]](#cite_note-245)

Although the territory had one of Asia's largest manufacturing economies during the latter half of the colonial era, Hong Kong's economy is now dominated by the service sector. The sector generates 92.7% of economic output, with the public sector accounting for about 10%.[[239]](#cite_note-246) Between 1961 and 1997, Hong Kong's gross domestic product increased by a factor of 180, and per capita GDP increased by a factor of 87.[[240]](#cite_note-247)[[241]](#cite_note-248) The territory's GDP relative to mainland China's peaked at 27% in 1993; it fell to less than 3% in 2017, as the mainland developed and liberalised its economy.[[242]](#cite_note-249) Economic and infrastructure integration with China has increased significantly since the 1978 start of the [reform and opening up](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up") on the mainland. Since resumption of [cross-boundary train service](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guangzhou–Kowloon_through_train "Guangzhou–Kowloon through train") in 1979, many rail and road links have been improved and constructed, facilitating trade between regions.[[243]](#cite_note-250)[[244]](#cite_note-HZMBridge-251) The [Closer Economic Partnership Arrangement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mainland_and_Hong_Kong_Closer_Economic_Partnership_Arrangement "Mainland and Hong Kong Closer Economic Partnership Arrangement") formalised a policy of free trade between the two areas, with each jurisdiction pledging to remove remaining obstacles to trade and cross-boundary investment.[[245]](#cite_note-MainlandCEPA-252) A similar economic partnership with Macau details the liberalisation of trade between the special administrative regions.[[246]](#cite_note-253) Chinese companies have expanded their economic presence in the territory since the handover. Mainland firms represent over half of the [Hang Seng Index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hang_Seng_Index "Hang Seng Index") value, up from 5% in 1997.[[247]](#cite_note-254)[[248]](#cite_note-255)

[![Large, empty room, with many desks and computer terminals](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Hong_Kong_Exchange_Trade_Lobby_2007.jpg/250px-Hong_Kong_Exchange_Trade_Lobby_2007.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_Exchange_Trade_Lobby_2007.jpg)

Former trading floor of the [Hong Kong Stock Exchange](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Stock_Exchange "Hong Kong Stock Exchange")

As the mainland economy liberalised, Hong Kong's shipping industry faced intense competition from other Chinese ports. Half of China's trade goods were routed through Hong Kong in 1997, dropping to about 13% by 2015.[[249]](#cite_note-Commons5R18-256) The territory's minimal taxation, common law system, and civil service attract overseas corporations wishing to establish a presence in Asia.[[249]](#cite_note-Commons5R18-256) The city has the second-highest number of corporate headquarters in the Asia-Pacific region.[[250]](#cite_note-257) Hong Kong is a gateway for [foreign direct investment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment") in China, giving investors open access to mainland Chinese markets through direct links with [the Shanghai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shanghai-Hong_Kong_Stock_Connect "Shanghai-Hong Kong Stock Connect") and [Shenzhen stock exchanges](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shenzhen-Hong_Kong_Stock_Connect "Shenzhen-Hong Kong Stock Connect"). The territory was the first market outside mainland China for [renminbi-denominated bonds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dim_sum_bond "Dim sum bond"), and is one of the largest hubs for offshore [renminbi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") trading.[[251]](#cite_note-258) In November 2020, Hong Kong's Financial Services and the Treasury Bureau proposed a new law that will restrict cryptocurrency trading to professional investors only, leaving amateur traders (93% of Hong Kong's trading population) out of the market.[[252]](#cite_note-259) The [Hong Kong dollar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_dollar "Hong Kong dollar"), the local currency, is the eighth-most traded currency in the world.[[253]](#cite_note-260) Due to extremely compact house sizes and the extremely high [housing density](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urban_density "Urban density"), the city has the [most expensive housing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_houses_in_Hong_Kong "List of most expensive houses in Hong Kong") market in the world.[[254]](#cite_note-261)[[255]](#cite_note-262)[[256]](#cite_note-263)

The government has had a passive role in the economy. Colonial governments had little [industrial policy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Industrial_policy "Industrial policy") and implemented almost no [trade controls](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trade_restriction "Trade restriction"). Under the doctrine of "[positive non-interventionism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positive_non-interventionism "Positive non-interventionism")", post-war administrations deliberately avoided direct resource allocation; active intervention was considered detrimental to economic growth.[[257]](#cite_note-ceo-264) While the economy transitioned to a service basis during the 1980s,[[257]](#cite_note-ceo-264) late colonial governments introduced interventionist policies. Post-handover administrations continued and expanded these programmes, including [export-credit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Export_credit_agency "Export credit agency") guarantees, a [compulsory pension scheme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mandatory_Provident_Fund "Mandatory Provident Fund"), a [minimum wage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minimum_Wage_Ordinance "Minimum Wage Ordinance"), [anti-discrimination laws](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anti-discrimination_law "Anti-discrimination law"), and a state mortgage backer.[[258]](#cite_note-EconomistEnd-265)

In April 2026, Hong Kong's Securities and Futures Commission launched a regulatory framework to enable secondary trading of tokenised investment products on licensed virtual asset platforms, aiming to broaden retail investors' access to regulated digital asset trading services.[[259]](#cite_note-266)

### Tourism

Tourism is a major part of Hong Kong's economy, accounting for 5% of its GDP.[[194]](#cite_note-TourismFacts-201) In 2016, 26.6 million visitors contributed HK$258 billion (US$32.9 billion) to the territory, making Hong Kong the [14th-most popular destination for international tourists](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings").[*[needs update](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] It is the most popular Chinese city for tourists, receiving over 70% more visitors than its closest competitor [Macau](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Macau "Macau").[[260]](#cite_note-267) The city is ranked as one of the [most expensive cities for expatriates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_cities_for_expatriate_employees "List of most expensive cities for expatriate employees").[[261]](#cite_note-268)[[262]](#cite_note-269) However, since 2020, there has been a sharp decline in incoming visitors due to tight COVID-19 travel restrictions. In an attempt to attract tourists back to Hong Kong, the Hong Kong government announced plans to give away 500,000 free airline tickets in 2023.[[263]](#cite_note-270) Hong Kong was the second most visited city in the world with 23.2 million visitors in 2025.[[264]](#cite_note-271)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Hong_Kong_Skyline_view_from_the_peak_2017.jpg/960px-Hong_Kong_Skyline_view_from_the_peak_2017.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_Skyline_view_from_the_peak_2017.jpg "Victoria Peak is a major tourist attraction that offers views of Central and Victoria Harbour.")

[Victoria Peak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victoria_Peak "Victoria Peak") is a major tourist attraction that offers views of [Central](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central,_Hong_Kong "Central, Hong Kong") and [Victoria Harbour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victoria_Harbour "Victoria Harbour").

## Infrastructure

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transport_in_Hong_Kong "Transport in Hong Kong")

[![Tunnel entrance at night, with heavy traffic](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Kowloon_Waterfront%2C_Hong_Kong%2C_2013-08-09%2C_DD_05.jpg/250px-Kowloon_Waterfront%2C_Hong_Kong%2C_2013-08-09%2C_DD_05.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Kowloon_Waterfront,_Hong_Kong,_2013-08-09,_DD_05.jpg)

[Hong Kong taxis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taxis_of_Hong_Kong "Taxis of Hong Kong")

Hong Kong has a highly developed and sophisticated transport network. Over 90% of daily trips made by its residents are made with public transport, the highest percentage in the world.[[265]](#cite_note-pubtransport-272) The [Octopus card](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Octopus_card "Octopus card"), a [contactless](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contactless_payment "Contactless payment") [smart](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Smart_card "Smart card") [payment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_currency "Digital currency") card made for Hong Kong, is widely accepted on railways, trams, buses and ferries, and can also be used for payment in most retail stores.[[266]](#cite_note-273) Alternative payments such as [Apple Pay](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Pay "Apple Pay"), [AliPay](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alipay "Alipay"), [Mastercard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mastercard "Mastercard") and [Visa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visa_Inc. "Visa Inc.") have also been gradually introduced as methods of payment for public transport.[[267]](#cite_note-274)[[268]](#cite_note-275)[[269]](#cite_note-276)[[270]](#cite_note-277)[[271]](#cite_note-278)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Viewed_from_the_Trams_Hong_Kong._%288619973955%29.jpg/330px-Viewed_from_the_Trams_Hong_Kong._%288619973955%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Viewed_from_the_Trams_Hong_Kong._(8619973955).jpg)

Hong Kong buses and trams

Hong Kong's EV policy, driven by the "Hong Kong Roadmap on Popularisation of Electric Vehicles," aims to achieve zero vehicular emissions before 2050. Key strategies include banning new registration of fuel-propelled private cars, including hybrids, by 2035 or earlier, expanding charging infrastructure, and offering significant, extended tax concessions such as the One-for-One Replacement Scheme (expiring 31 March 2026).[[272]](#cite_note-279)

#### Land

The [Peak Tram](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peak_Tram "Peak Tram"), Hong Kong's first public transport system, has provided [funicular](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Funicular "Funicular") rail transport between [Central](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central,_Hong_Kong "Central, Hong Kong") and [Victoria Peak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victoria_Peak "Victoria Peak") since 1888.[[273]](#cite_note-280)

The [Central and Western District](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_and_Western_District "Central and Western District") has an extensive system of escalators and [moving pavements](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moving_walkway "Moving walkway"), which, together being the [Mid-Levels escalator system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central–Mid-Levels_escalator "Central–Mid-Levels escalator"), is the world's longest outdoor covered escalator system.[[274]](#cite_note-281)

[Hong Kong Tramways](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Tramways "Hong Kong Tramways")' tram network covers a portion of Hong Kong Island, covering from [Kennedy Town](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kennedy_Town "Kennedy Town") to [Shau Kei Wan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shau_Kei_Wan "Shau Kei Wan"), with a branch to [Happy Valley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Happy_Valley,_Hong_Kong "Happy Valley, Hong Kong"). It operates 6 routes and has had a ridership of 42,558 in 2022.[[275]](#cite_note-282)[[276]](#cite_note-283) It began servicing Hong Kong since 1904.[[277]](#cite_note-284) Hong Kong Tramways currently holds the Guinness World Record as the "Largest double-decker tram fleet in service", certified on 30 July 2021, with a fleet of 165 double-decker trams.[[278]](#cite_note-285) The fleet of trams was mostly built by Hong Kong Tramways.

The [Mass Transit Railway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MTR "MTR") (MTR) is an extensive passenger rail network, connecting 99 [metro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") stations and 68 [light-rail](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light_Rail_(MTR) "Light Rail (MTR)") stops throughout the territory.[[279]](#cite_note-RailwayFacts-286)[[280]](#cite_note-287) With a daily ridership of almost five million, the system serves 41% of all public transit passengers in the city[[281]](#cite_note-LegcoTransport-288) and has an on-time rate of 99.9%.[[282]](#cite_note-289)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Farewell_Hong_Kong_5th_gen_Peak_Tram_%2851282280992%29.jpg/330px-Farewell_Hong_Kong_5th_gen_Peak_Tram_%2851282280992%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Farewell_Hong_Kong_5th_gen_Peak_Tram_(51282280992).jpg)

[Hong Kong Peak Tram](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peak_Tram "Peak Tram")

Train service to [Shenzhen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") is offered by the [East Rail line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/East_Rail_line "East Rail line"), and longer-distance [inter-city trains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail") [to Guangzhou](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guangzhou–Kowloon_through_train "Guangzhou–Kowloon through train"), [Shanghai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shanghai–Kowloon_through_train "Shanghai–Kowloon through train"), [Chongqing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"), [Beijing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beijing–Kowloon_through_train "Beijing–Kowloon through train") are operated from [Hung Hom station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hung_Hom_station "Hung Hom station").[[283]](#cite_note-290) [Connecting service](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Express_Rail_Link "Hong Kong Express Rail Link") to the [national high-speed rail system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/China_Railway_High-speed "China Railway High-speed") is provided at [West Kowloon railway station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_West_Kowloon_railway_station "Hong Kong West Kowloon railway station").[[284]](#cite_note-291)

[![Light-rail train on a straightaway](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/V813_entering_Sunny_Bay_Station_%2820181013082946%29.jpg/250px-V813_entering_Sunny_Bay_Station_%2820181013082946%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:V813_entering_Sunny_Bay_Station_(20181013082946).jpg)

MTR train on the [Tung Chung line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tung_Chung_line "Tung Chung line")

Although public transport systems handle most passenger traffic, over 500,000 private vehicles are registered in Hong Kong.[[285]](#cite_note-292) Unlike in mainland China, automobiles in Hong Kong drive [on the left](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Left-_and_right-hand_traffic "Left- and right-hand traffic") due to the historical influence of the British Empire.[[286]](#cite_note-293) Vehicle traffic is extremely congested in urban areas, exacerbated by limited space to expand roads and an increasing number of vehicles.[[287]](#cite_note-294) More than 18,000 [taxicabs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taxis_of_Hong_Kong "Taxis of Hong Kong"), easily identifiable by their bright colours and taxi lights, are licensed to carry riders in the territory.[[288]](#cite_note-295) Unlicensed ride-hailing services such as [Uber](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uber "Uber") exist in Hong Kong as well, with some operating with licensed taxis to legitimise their business in ride-sharing. Unlicensed drivers have been targeted by the government and taxi drivers in the past, mainly due to the lack of [third-party insurance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Third-party_insurance "Third-party insurance") for passengers, and because taxi drivers feared competition from drivers of these ride-hailing services.[[289]](#cite_note-296)[[290]](#cite_note-297) In 2024, the government looked into legalising these services.[[291]](#cite_note-298)[Bus services](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bus_services_in_Hong_Kong "Bus services in Hong Kong") operate more than 700 routes across the territory,[[281]](#cite_note-LegcoTransport-288) with smaller [public light buses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_light_bus "Public light bus") (also known as minibuses) serving areas standard buses do not reach as frequently or directly.[[292]](#cite_note-299) Expressways and truck roads, organised with the [Hong Kong Strategic Route and Exit Number System](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Strategic_Route_and_Exit_Number_System "Hong Kong Strategic Route and Exit Number System"), connect all major areas of the territory.[[293]](#cite_note-300) The [Hong Kong–Zhuhai–Macau Bridge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong–Zhuhai–Macau_Bridge "Hong Kong–Zhuhai–Macau Bridge") provides a direct route to the western side of the Pearl River estuary.[[244]](#cite_note-HZMBridge-251)

#### Air

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Aeropuerto_de_Hong_Kong%2C_2013-08-13%2C_DD_02.JPG/330px-Aeropuerto_de_Hong_Kong%2C_2013-08-13%2C_DD_02.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Aeropuerto_de_Hong_Kong,_2013-08-13,_DD_02.JPG)

[Hong Kong International Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_Airport "Hong Kong International Airport")

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Cathay_Pacific_B777-300ER_%28B-KPP%29_%40_HKG%2C_March_2019.jpg/330px-Cathay_Pacific_B777-300ER_%28B-KPP%29_%40_HKG%2C_March_2019.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Cathay_Pacific_B777-300ER_(B-KPP)_@_HKG,_March_2019.jpg)

[Cathay Pacific](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cathay_Pacific "Cathay Pacific"), Hong Kong's [Flagcarrier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier")

[Hong Kong International Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_Airport "Hong Kong International Airport") is the territory's primary airport, replacing [Kai Tak International Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kai_Tak_Airport "Kai Tak Airport") which ended operations in 1998.[[294]](#cite_note-301)

Over 100 airlines operate flights from the airport, including [Cathay Pacific](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cathay_Pacific "Cathay Pacific"), the [flag carrier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") of Hong Kong. It has been regarded as a 5-star airline by Skytrax, and been in the world top 10 list of airlines for multiple consecutive years. [Hong Kong Airlines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Airlines "Hong Kong Airlines"), low-cost airline [HK Express](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HK_Express "HK Express"), low-cost airline [Greater Bay Airlines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greater_Bay_Airlines "Greater Bay Airlines"), and cargo airline [Air Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Air_Hong_Kong "Air Hong Kong") are all airlines based in Hong Kong.[[295]](#cite_note-302)

Hong Kong International Airport was the [eighth-busiest airport by passenger traffic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic "List of busiest airports by passenger traffic")[[296]](#cite_note-303) pre-COVID and handles [the most air-cargo traffic in the world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_cargo_traffic "List of busiest airports by cargo traffic").[[297]](#cite_note-304)

Most private recreational aviation in Hong Kong, flies through [Shek Kong Airfield](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shek_Kong_Airfield "Shek Kong Airfield"), under the supervision of the [Hong Kong Aviation Club](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Aviation_Club "Hong Kong Aviation Club").[[298]](#cite_note-305)

#### Sea

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Tsim_Sha_Tsui_Ferry_Pier.jpg/330px-Tsim_Sha_Tsui_Ferry_Pier.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Tsim_Sha_Tsui_Ferry_Pier.jpg)

Tsim Sha Tsui Ferry Pier

The [Port of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Port_of_Hong_Kong "Port of Hong Kong") located by the [South China Sea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea"), is a deepwater [seaport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seaport "Seaport") dominated by trade in [containerised](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Containerisation "Containerisation") manufactured products. A key factor in the economic development of Hong Kong, the natural shelter and deep waters of [Victoria Harbour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Victoria_Harbour "Victoria Harbour") provide ideal conditions for berthing and the handling of all types of vessels. It is one of the [busiest ports](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World's_busiest_port "World's busiest port") in the world, in the three categories of shipping movements, cargo handled and passengers carried. This makes Hong Kong a Large-Port Metropolis.[[299]](#cite_note-tss-306) The [Star Ferry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star_Ferry "Star Ferry") operates two lines across Victoria Harbour for its 53,000 daily passengers.[[300]](#cite_note-307) Ferries also serve outlying islands inaccessible by other means. Smaller [kai-to](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kai-to "Kai-to") boats serve the most remote coastal settlements.[[301]](#cite_note-308) Ferry travel to Macau and mainland China is also available.[[302]](#cite_note-309) [Junks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Junk_(ship) "Junk (ship)"), once common in Hong Kong waters, are no longer widely available and are used privately and for tourism.[[303]](#cite_note-310) The large size of the port gives Hong Kong the classification of Large-Port Metropolis.[[299]](#cite_note-tss-306)

### Utilities

Main articles: [Energy in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy_in_Hong_Kong "Energy in Hong Kong"), [Electricity sector in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electricity_sector_in_Hong_Kong "Electricity sector in Hong Kong"), and [Water supply and sanitation in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Hong_Kong "Water supply and sanitation in Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Overlook_Lamma_Power_Station.jpg/250px-Overlook_Lamma_Power_Station.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Overlook_Lamma_Power_Station.jpg)

[Lamma Power Station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lamma_Power_Station "Lamma Power Station"), a coal power plant on [Lamma Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lamma_Island "Lamma Island") owned by [Hongkong Electric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hongkong_Electric_Company "Hongkong Electric Company")

Hong Kong generates most of its electricity locally.[[304]](#cite_note-energystats2016p1-311) The vast majority of this energy comes from fossil fuels, with 46% from coal and 47% from petroleum.[[305]](#cite_note-312) The rest is from other imports, including nuclear energy generated in mainland China.[[306]](#cite_note-313) Renewable sources account for a negligible amount of energy generated for the territory.[[307]](#cite_note-314) Small-scale wind-power sources have been developed,[[304]](#cite_note-energystats2016p1-311) and a small number of private homes and public buildings have installed solar panels.[[308]](#cite_note-315)

With few natural lakes and rivers, high population density, inaccessible groundwater sources, and extremely seasonal rainfall, the territory lacks a reliable freshwater supply. The [Dong River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dong_River_(China) "Dong River (China)") in Guangdong supplies 70% of the city's water,[[309]](#cite_note-LeeWater-316) with the remaining demand met by harvesting rainwater locally.[[310]](#cite_note-317) Toilets in most built-up areas of the territory flush with seawater, which reduces freshwater use.[[309]](#cite_note-LeeWater-316)

[Broadband](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Broadband "Broadband") Internet access is widely available, with 99.3% of households connected. Connections over [fibre-optic infrastructure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fiber_to_the_x "Fiber to the x") are increasingly prevalent,[[311]](#cite_note-318) contributing to the high regional average connection speed of 21.9 Mbit/s (the world's fourth-fastest).[[312]](#cite_note-319) Mobile-phone use is ubiquitous;[[313]](#cite_note-320) there are almost 22 million [mobile-phone accounts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_mobile_phones_in_use "List of countries by number of mobile phones in use") registered in Hong Kong,[[314]](#cite_note-321) which is almost triple the territory's population.

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Culture_of_Hong_Kong "Culture of Hong Kong")

See also: [Cantonese culture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese_culture "Cantonese culture")

Hong Kong culture is characterised as a hybrid of [Eastern](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eastern_world "Eastern world") and [Western](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture") culture. Traditional Chinese values emphasising family and education blend with Western ideals, including economic liberty and the rule of law.[[315]](#cite_note-322) Although the vast majority of the population is ethnically Chinese, Hong Kong has developed a distinct identity. The territory diverged from the mainland through its long period of colonial administration and a different pace of economic, social, and cultural development. Mainstream culture was derived from immigrants originating from various parts of China; it was then influenced by British-style education, a separate political system, and the territory's rapid development during the late 20th century.[[316]](#cite_note-323)[[317]](#cite_note-324) Most migrants of that era fled poverty and war, reflected in the prevailing attitude toward wealth; Hongkongers would tend to link self-image and decision-making to material benefits.[[318]](#cite_note-325)[[319]](#cite_note-326) Residents' sense of local identity has increased post-handover: polling in December 2022 had 32% of respondents identifying as "Hongkongers", 34.1% identifying as "Hongkongers in China" 45.9% purporting a "Mixed Identity", 20.5% identifying as "Chinese" and 11.9% identifying as "Chinese in Hong Kong".[[320]](#cite_note-327)

Traditional Chinese family values, including [family honour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Family_honor "Family honor"), [filial piety](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Filial_piety "Filial piety"), and a [preference for sons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sex_selection "Sex selection"), are prevalent.[[321]](#cite_note-328) [Nuclear families](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuclear_family "Nuclear family") are the most common households, although multi-generational and extended families are not unusual.[[322]](#cite_note-329) Spiritual concepts such as *[feng shui](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Feng_shui "Feng shui")* are observed; large-scale construction projects often hire consultants to ensure proper building positioning and layout. The degree of its adherence to *feng shui* is believed to determine a business's success.[[190]](#cite_note-FengShui-197) *[Bagua](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bagua "Bagua")* mirrors are regularly used to deflect evil spirits,[[323]](#cite_note-330) and buildings often lack [floor numbers with a 4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_numerology "Chinese numerology");[[324]](#cite_note-331) the number has a similar sound to the word for "die" in Cantonese.[[325]](#cite_note-332)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Hong Kong cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_cuisine "Hong Kong cuisine")

[![An assortment of items in a Dim Sum breakfast meal](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Dim_Sum_Breakfast.jpg/250px-Dim_Sum_Breakfast.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Dim_Sum_Breakfast.jpg)

[![French Toast on left, Milk Tea on right](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Cha_Chaan_Teng.jpg/250px-Cha_Chaan_Teng.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Cha_Chaan_Teng.jpg)

Typical fare at a dim sum restaurant (left); *[cha chaan teng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cha_chaan_teng "Cha chaan teng")* breakfast food with [Hong Kong-style milk tea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong-style_milk_tea "Hong Kong-style milk tea") (right)

Food in Hong Kong is primarily based on [Cantonese cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese_cuisine "Cantonese cuisine"), despite the territory's exposure to foreign influences and its residents' varied origins. Rice is the staple food, and is usually served plain with other dishes.[[326]](#cite_note-LongHKFood-333) Freshness of ingredients is emphasised. Poultry and seafood are commonly sold live at [wet markets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wet_market "Wet market"), and ingredients are used as quickly as possible when still fresh.[[327]](#cite_note-334) There are up to five daily meals: breakfast, lunch, afternoon tea, dinner, and *[siu yeh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Siu_yeh "Siu yeh")*.[[328]](#cite_note-335) [Dim sum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dim_sum "Dim sum"), as part of *[yum cha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yum_cha "Yum cha")* (brunch), is a dining-out tradition with family and friends. Dishes include [congee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Congee "Congee"), *[cha siu bao](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cha_siu_bao "Cha siu bao")*, *[siu yuk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Siu_yuk "Siu yuk")*, [egg tarts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Egg_tart "Egg tart"), and [mango pudding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mango_pudding "Mango pudding"). Local versions of Western food are served at *[cha chaan teng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cha_chaan_teng "Cha chaan teng")* (Hong Kong-style cafes). Common *cha chaan teng* menu items include macaroni in soup, deep-fried French toast, and [Hong Kong-style milk tea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong-style_milk_tea "Hong Kong-style milk tea").[[326]](#cite_note-LongHKFood-333)

As of 2025, the [Michelin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michelin_guide "Michelin guide") Guide listed 76 starred restaurants in total. It awarded 7 restaurants 3 Michelin Stars, 11 restaurants with 2 Michelin Stars, and 58 restaurants with 1 Michelin Star. It also gave 4 Green Stars for sustainability, Amber, Feuille, Mora, and Roganic. Alongside the starred list, 65 restaurants received [Bib Gourmand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michelin_Guide "Michelin Guide") status for good quality food at good value.

### Language

Main article: [Languages of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Languages_of_Hong_Kong "Languages of Hong Kong")

The predominant language today is [Cantonese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese"), a [variety of Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") originating in Guangzhou. It is spoken by 93.7% of the population, 88.2% as a first language and 5.5% as a second language.[[3]](#cite_note-2021By-CensusLanguages-3) Slightly over half the population (58.7%) speaks [English](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/English_language "English language"), the other official language;[[2]](#cite_note-OfficialLanguagesOrd-2) 4.6% are native speakers, and 54.1% speak English as a second language.[[3]](#cite_note-2021By-CensusLanguages-3) [Code-switching](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Code-switching_in_Hong_Kong "Code-switching in Hong Kong"), mixing English and Cantonese in informal conversation, is common among the bilingual population.[[329]](#cite_note-336) Post-handover governments have promoted [Mandarin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese"), which is currently about as prevalent as English; 54.2% of the population speaks Mandarin, with 2.3% native speakers and 51.9% as a second language.[[3]](#cite_note-2021By-CensusLanguages-3) [Traditional Chinese characters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters") are used in writing, rather than the [simplified characters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters") used in the [mainland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China").[[330]](#cite_note-337)

Before the [First Opium War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War"), Hong Kong had no significant [Cantonese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese")-speaking population. Instead, most people spoke [Hakka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese") varieties. However, large areas with speakers of other [Yue Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese") varieties, namely [Weitou](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weitou_dialect "Weitou dialect") and [Tanka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tanka_dialect "Tanka dialect"), speakers can be found in the northern New Territories and southern coastal areas, respectively. [Hong Kong Hakka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Hakka "Hong Kong Hakka") is a variety of Neo-Hakka, and belongs to the [Mei-Hui Hakka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mei-Hui_Hakka?action=edit&redlink=1 "Mei-Hui Hakka (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/梅惠小片 "zh:梅惠小片")] subbranch of [Yuetai Hakka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yuetai_Hakka?action=edit&redlink=1 "Yuetai Hakka (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/粵台客語 "zh:粵台客語")], making it closely related to that of [Meixian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meixian_dialect "Meixian dialect").[[331]](#cite_note-338) Weitou Yue is a variety that is closely related to the [Yue Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese") varieties spoken in [Bao'an](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bao'an,_Shenzhen "Bao'an, Shenzhen") and [Dongguan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dongguan "Dongguan"), and was the primary language of the [Five Great Clans of the New Territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Five_Great_Clans_of_the_New_Territories "Five Great Clans of the New Territories"), brought into Hong Kong during the [Song dynasty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") from [Jiangxi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jiangxi "Jiangxi").[[332]](#cite_note-distributionNT-339) Some of the native Yue Chinese varieties were noticeably Hakka-influenced.[[333]](#cite_note-340) Smaller communities of [Hokkien](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien") speakers also existed, and many villages hosted more than one ethnolinguistic group.[[332]](#cite_note-distributionNT-339)

Today, communities of speakers of other Chinese varieties (such as [Teochew](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Teochew_Min "Teochew Min"), [Sze Yap](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Siyi_Yue "Siyi Yue"), [Hokkien](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien"), and [Shanghainese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shanghainese "Shanghainese")) also live in Hong Kong. Southeast Asian languages such as [Tagalog](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language") and [Malay-Indonesian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") speaking communities also live in Hong Kong. Other minority languages with significant speaker bases in Hong Kong include [French](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Dutch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"), [German](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_language "German language"), [Italian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), [Spanish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [Portuguese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), [Hindustani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language"), [Japanese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"), and [Korean](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"), among others.[[334]](#cite_note-341)

### Cinema

Main article: [Cinema of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cinema_of_Hong_Kong "Cinema of Hong Kong")

[![Statue of Bruce Lee in a fighting pose](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Bruce_Lee%2C_Avenue_of_Stars%2C_Hong_Kong_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Bruce_Lee%2C_Avenue_of_Stars%2C_Hong_Kong_-_panoramio.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Bruce_Lee,_Avenue_of_Stars,_Hong_Kong_-_panoramio.jpg)

Statue of [Bruce Lee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee") on the [Avenue of Stars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Avenue_of_Stars,_Hong_Kong "Avenue of Stars, Hong Kong"), a tribute to the city's film industry

Hong Kong developed into a filmmaking hub during the late 1940s as a wave of Shanghai filmmakers migrated to the territory, and these movie veterans helped build the colony's entertainment industry over the next decade.[[335]](#cite_note-342) By the 1960s, the city was well known to overseas audiences through films such as *[The World of Suzie Wong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_World_of_Suzie_Wong_(film) "The World of Suzie Wong (film)")*.[[336]](#cite_note-343) When [Bruce Lee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee")'s *[The Way of the Dragon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Way_of_the_Dragon "The Way of the Dragon")* released in 1972 and [Enter the Dragon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enter_the_Dragon "Enter the Dragon") in 1973, local productions became popular outside Hong Kong. During the 1980s and 1900s, films such as *[A Better Tomorrow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/A_Better_Tomorrow "A Better Tomorrow")*, *[As Tears Go By](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/As_Tears_Go_By_(film) "As Tears Go By (film)")*, *[God of Gamblers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/God_of_Gamblers "God of Gamblers")*, *[Police Story](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Police_Story_(1985_film) "Police Story (1985 film)")* and *[Zu Warriors from the Magic Mountain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zu_Warriors_from_the_Magic_Mountain "Zu Warriors from the Magic Mountain")* expanded global interest beyond [martial arts films](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martial_arts_film "Martial arts film"); locally made gangster films, romantic dramas, and supernatural fantasies became popular.[[337]](#cite_note-344)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Jackie_Chan_Cannes.jpg/250px-Jackie_Chan_Cannes.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Jackie_Chan_Cannes.jpg)

[Jackie Chan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan")

Hong Kong cinema continued to be internationally successful over the following decades with critically acclaimed movies and dramas such as *[Farewell My Concubine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Farewell_My_Concubine_(film) "Farewell My Concubine (film)")*, *[To Live](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/To_Live_(1994_film) "To Live (1994 film)")*, *[Shaolin Soccer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shaolin_Soccer "Shaolin Soccer")*, *[Kung Fu Hustle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kung_Fu_Hustle "Kung Fu Hustle")*, *[Ip Man](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ip_Man_(film_series) "Ip Man (film series)")* *,[Drunken Master II](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Drunken_Master_II "Drunken Master II")*, *[In the Mood for Love](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/In_the_Mood_for_Love "In the Mood for Love")* and *[Chungking Express](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chungking_Express "Chungking Express")*. The city's martial arts film roots are evident in the roles of the most prolific Hong Kong actors. [Bruce Lee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee"), [Jackie Chan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan"), [Stephen Chow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stephen_Chow "Stephen Chow"), [Jet Li](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jet_Li "Jet Li"), [Chow Yun-fat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chow_Yun-fat "Chow Yun-fat"), [Donnie Yen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Donnie_Yen "Donnie Yen"), [Tony Leung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tony_Leung_Chiu-wai "Tony Leung Chiu-wai"), [Karen Mok](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karen_Mok "Karen Mok"), [Maggie Q](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maggie_Q "Maggie Q") and [Michelle Yeoh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michelle_Yeoh "Michelle Yeoh") are some of the most popular and well known actors and actresses. Hong Kong films have also grown popular in overseas markets such as Japan, South Korea, Indonesia, Taiwan and the US, earning the city the moniker "Hollywood of the East".[[338]](#cite_note-345) At the height of the local movie industry in the early 1990s, over 400 films were produced each year; since then, industry momentum has shifted to mainland China. The number of films produced annually has declined to about 60 in 2017.[[339]](#cite_note-346)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Music_of_Hong_Kong "Music of Hong Kong")

[![Leslie Cheung with a microphone](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Leslie_Cheung_in_Madame_Tussauds_Hong_Kong_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Leslie_Cheung_in_Madame_Tussauds_Hong_Kong_%28cropped%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Leslie_Cheung_in_Madame_Tussauds_Hong_Kong_(cropped).jpg)

[![A serious-looking Andy Lau, seated and wearing a suit](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Andy_Lau_%E5%88%98%E5%BE%B7%E5%8D%8E%2C_Beijing_International_Film_Festival_%E5%8C%97%E4%BA%AC%E7%94%B5%E5%BD%B1%E8%8A%82%2C_2013_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Andy_Lau_%E5%88%98%E5%BE%B7%E5%8D%8E%2C_Beijing_International_Film_Festival_%E5%8C%97%E4%BA%AC%E7%94%B5%E5%BD%B1%E8%8A%82%2C_2013_%28cropped%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Andy_Lau_刘德华,_Beijing_International_Film_Festival_北京电影节,_2013_(cropped).jpg)

[Leslie Cheung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leslie_Cheung "Leslie Cheung") (left) is considered a pioneering Cantopop artist, and [Andy Lau](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andy_Lau "Andy Lau") has been an icon of Hong Kong music and film for several decades as a member of the [Four Heavenly Kings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Four_Heavenly_Kings_(Hong_Kong) "Four Heavenly Kings (Hong Kong)").

[Cantopop](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantopop "Cantopop") is a genre of Cantonese popular music which emerged in Hong Kong during the 1970s. Evolving from Shanghai-style *[shidaiqu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shidaiqu "Shidaiqu")*, it is also influenced by [Cantonese opera](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese_opera "Cantonese opera") and Western pop.[[340]](#cite_note-347) Local media featured songs by artists and bands such as [Sam Hui](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sam_Hui "Sam Hui"), [Anita Mui](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anita_Mui "Anita Mui"), [Leslie Cheung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leslie_Cheung "Leslie Cheung"), [Alan Tam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alan_Tam "Alan Tam"), [Hacken Lee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hacken_Lee "Hacken Lee") and [Beyond](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beyond_(band) "Beyond (band)") ; during the 1980s, exported films and shows exposed Cantopop to a global audience.[[341]](#cite_note-348) The genre's popularity peaked in the 1990s, when the [Four Heavenly Kings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Four_Heavenly_Kings_(Hong_Kong) "Four Heavenly Kings (Hong Kong)") dominated Asian record charts.[[342]](#cite_note-349) Despite a general decline since late in the decade,[[343]](#cite_note-350) Cantopop remains dominant in Hong Kong; contemporary artists such as [Eason Chan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eason_Chan "Eason Chan"), [Jackson Wang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jackson_Wang "Jackson Wang"), [Joey Yung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joey_Yung "Joey Yung"), [G.E.M.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G.E.M. "G.E.M."), [Tyson Yoshi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tyson_Yoshi "Tyson Yoshi"), [Twins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Twins_(group) "Twins (group)") and [Mirror](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mirror_(group) "Mirror (group)") are popular in the territory and around the world.[[344]](#cite_note-351)

Western classical music has historically had a strong presence in Hong Kong and remains a large part of local musical education.[[345]](#cite_note-352) The publicly funded [Hong Kong Philharmonic Orchestra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Hong Kong Philharmonic Orchestra"), the territory's oldest professional symphony orchestra, and frequently hosts musicians and conductors from all over the world. The [Hong Kong Chinese Orchestra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Chinese_Orchestra "Hong Kong Chinese Orchestra"), composed of [classical Chinese instruments](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_orchestra "Chinese orchestra"), is the leading Chinese ensemble and plays a significant role in promoting traditional music in the city.[[346]](#cite_note-353)

Hong Kong has never had a separate national anthem from the country that controlled it; its current official national anthem is therefore that of the People's Republic of China, "[March of the Volunteers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/March_of_the_Volunteers "March of the Volunteers")". The song "[Glory to Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glory_to_Hong_Kong "Glory to Hong Kong")" has been used by [protestors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2019–2020_Hong_Kong_protests "2019–2020 Hong Kong protests") as an unofficial anthem of the city.[[347]](#cite_note-354)[[348]](#cite_note-355)

### Galleries and museums

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/WKCD_M%2B%2C_2021.11.jpg/250px-WKCD_M%2B%2C_2021.11.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:WKCD_M+,_2021.11.jpg)

**[M+](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M+ "M+")**

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Hong_Kong_Art_Museum%EF%BC%882022%EF%BC%89.jpg/250px-Hong_Kong_Art_Museum%EF%BC%882022%EF%BC%89.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_Art_Museum（2022）.jpg)

[Hong Kong Museum of Art](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Museum_of_Art "Hong Kong Museum of Art")

Hong Kong is home to a wide range of museums, galleries, and cultural institutions, and is regarded as one of Asia's leading centres for art, heritage, and visual culture. Major cultural development in the city has taken place in the [West Kowloon Cultural District](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Kowloon_Cultural_District "West Kowloon Cultural District"), a large arts and museum quarter on reclaimed land in Kowloon. Its best known institutions include [M+](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M+ "M+"), a museum of visual culture focused on 20th and 21st century art, design, architecture, and moving image, and the [Hong Kong Palace Museum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Palace_Museum "Hong Kong Palace Museum"), which displays Chinese artworks and artefacts. Apart from West Kowloon, Hong Kong has many additional museums across the territory. The [Hong Kong Museum of Art](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Museum_of_Art "Hong Kong Museum of Art") in [Tsim Sha Tsui](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tsim_Sha_Tsui "Tsim Sha Tsui") houses collections of Chinese antiquities, calligraphy, painting, and Hong Kong art. The [Hong Kong Heritage Museum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Heritage_Museum "Hong Kong Heritage Museum") in [Sha Tin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sha_Tin "Sha Tin") focuses on history, art, and local popular culture, with exhibitions on Cantonese opera, film, and design. The [Hong Kong Museum of History](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Museum_of_History "Hong Kong Museum of History") presents the development of Hong Kong from prehistoric times to the present, while the [Hong Kong Science Museum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Science_Museum "Hong Kong Science Museum") and [Hong Kong Space Museum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Space_Museum "Hong Kong Space Museum") are among the city's main public institutions devoted to science and education. [Tai Kwun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tai_Kwun "Tai Kwun"), a former police station and prison compound in [Central](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central,_Hong_Kong "Central, Hong Kong"), has been revitalised as a centre for heritage and contemporary art, and hosts exhibitions, performances, and public programmes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Tai_Kwun_Parade_Ground_201806.jpg/330px-Tai_Kwun_Parade_Ground_201806.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Tai_Kwun_Parade_Ground_201806.jpg)

[Tai Kwun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tai_Kwun "Tai Kwun")

Hong Kong also has a large commercial and independent gallery scene. [Central district](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central,_Hong_Kong "Central, Hong Kong") has long been the city's main gallery district, with international auction houses and global galleries operating alongside local art spaces. [Wong Chuk Hang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wong_Chuk_Hang "Wong Chuk Hang") has developed into another important arts area, with former industrial buildings converted into galleries, studios, and exhibition venues. The city hosts major international art events, including [Art Basel Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Art_Basel "Art Basel") and Art Central, which attract galleries, collectors, and artists from around the world.

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sport_in_Hong_Kong "Sport in Hong Kong")

[![Cheering rugby fans, seen from the stands](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Crowd_cheering%2C_Hong_Kong_Sevens_2009.jpg/250px-Crowd_cheering%2C_Hong_Kong_Sevens_2009.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Crowd_cheering,_Hong_Kong_Sevens_2009.jpg)

The [Hong Kong Sevens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Sevens "Hong Kong Sevens"), considered the premier tournament of the [World Rugby Sevens Series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Rugby_Sevens_Series "World Rugby Sevens Series")

The city has hosted numerous major sporting events, including the inaugural [AFC Asian Cup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AFC_Asian_Cup "AFC Asian Cup"), the [2008 Summer Olympics equestrian events](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equestrian_at_the_2008_Summer_Olympics "Equestrian at the 2008 Summer Olympics"), the [2009 East Asian Games](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2009_East_Asian_Games "2009 East Asian Games"), the [Rugby World Cup Sevens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup_Sevens "Rugby World Cup Sevens") in 1997 and 2005, the 2005 [World Squash Championships](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Squash_Championships "World Squash Championships") and [2025 World Grand Prix (snooker)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025_World_Grand_Prix_(snooker) "2025 World Grand Prix (snooker)").[[349]](#cite_note-ShenKee-356)

The territory regularly hosts the [Hong Kong Sevens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Sevens "Hong Kong Sevens"), [Hong Kong Marathon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Marathon "Hong Kong Marathon"), [Hong Kong Open (tennis)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Open_(tennis) "Hong Kong Open (tennis)"), [Hong Kong Tennis Classic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Tennis_Classic "Hong Kong Tennis Classic") and [LIV Golf Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LIV_Golf_Hong_Kong "LIV Golf Hong Kong"). Hong Kong represents itself separately from mainland China, with its own sports teams in international competitions.[[349]](#cite_note-ShenKee-356) The territory has participated in almost every Summer Olympics since 1952 (with the exception of its support of the [1980 Summer Olympics boycott](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics_boycott "1980 Summer Olympics boycott")) and has earned [nine medals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_at_the_Olympics "Hong Kong at the Olympics"). [Lee Lai-shan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lee_Lai-shan "Lee Lai-shan") won the territory's first Olympic gold medal at the [1996 Atlanta Olympics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics"),[[350]](#cite_note-357) and [Cheung Ka Long](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cheung_Ka_Long "Cheung Ka Long") won the second one in [Tokyo 2020](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics").[[351]](#cite_note-358) Hong Kong athletes have won [126 medals at the Paralympic Games](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_at_the_Paralympics "Hong Kong at the Paralympics") and [17 at the Commonwealth Games](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_at_the_Commonwealth_Games "Hong Kong at the Commonwealth Games"). Although they are no longer part of the [Commonwealth of Nations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), the city's last appearance was in [1994](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1994_Commonwealth_Games "1994 Commonwealth Games").[[352]](#cite_note-359)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Happy_Valley_Racecourse-001.jpg/330px-Happy_Valley_Racecourse-001.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Happy_Valley_Racecourse-001.jpg)

[Happy Valley Racecourse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Happy_Valley_Racecourse "Happy Valley Racecourse") and [Hong Kong Jockey Club](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Jockey_Club "Hong Kong Jockey Club")

Sporting leagues in Hong Kong include, [Hong Kong Premier League](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Premier_League "Hong Kong Premier League"), [Hong Kong Premiership (rugby union)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Premiership_(rugby_union) "Hong Kong Premiership (rugby union)"), [Hong Kong A1 Division Championship](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_A1_Division_Championship "Hong Kong A1 Division Championship") and the [Hong Kong Ice Hockey League](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Ice_Hockey_League "Hong Kong Ice Hockey League"). Famous local sporting clubs include [Hong Kong Football Club](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Football_Club "Hong Kong Football Club"), [South China Athletic Association](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_China_Athletic_Association "South China Athletic Association"), [Kitchee SC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kitchee_SC "Kitchee SC") and [Hong Kong Eastern](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Eastern "Hong Kong Eastern").

Hong Kong sports has been described as "Club Life". Some clubs have documented their history thoroughly, while others have disappeared along with their past.

* [Hong Kong Cricket Club](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Cricket_Club "Hong Kong Cricket Club")
* Craigengower Cricket Club
* Kowloon Cricket Club
* Kowloon Bowling Green Club
* Indian Recreation Club
* Chinese Recreation Club
* Filipino Club
* United Services Recreation Club
* Club de Recreio
* Club Lusitano
* [Royal Hong Kong Yacht Club](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Royal_Hong_Kong_Yacht_Club "Royal Hong Kong Yacht Club")
* Aberdeen Boat Club

[Dragon boat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dragon_boat "Dragon boat") races originated as a religious ceremony conducted during the annual [Tuen Ng Festival](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dragon_Boat_Festival "Dragon Boat Festival"). The race was revived as a modern sport as part of the [Tourism Board](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Tourism_Board "Hong Kong Tourism Board")'s efforts to promote Hong Kong's image abroad. The first modern competition was organised in 1976, and overseas teams began competing in the first international race in 1993.[[353]](#cite_note-360)

The [Hong Kong Jockey Club](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Jockey_Club "Hong Kong Jockey Club"), the territory's largest taxpayer,[[354]](#cite_note-JockeyClub-361) has a monopoly on gambling and provides over 7% of government revenue.[[355]](#cite_note-362) Three forms of gambling are legal in Hong Kong: lotteries, horse racing, and football.[[354]](#cite_note-JockeyClub-361)

### Entertainment and leisure

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/LKF_Xmas_02.jpg/250px-LKF_Xmas_02.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:LKF_Xmas_02.jpg)

[Lan Kwai Fong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lan_Kwai_Fong "Lan Kwai Fong")

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Hong_Kong_Disneyland_Main_Street_Vehicles_Taxi.jpg/330px-Hong_Kong_Disneyland_Main_Street_Vehicles_Taxi.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_Disneyland_Main_Street_Vehicles_Taxi.jpg)

[Hong Kong Disneyland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Disneyland "Hong Kong Disneyland")

Hong Kong has two theme parks. [Hong Kong Disneyland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Disneyland "Hong Kong Disneyland") on Lantau Island is a major theme park and resort destination. [Ocean Park Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ocean_Park_Hong_Kong "Ocean Park Hong Kong") combines rides and animal exhibits, with zones across the Waterfront and Summit areas.

For big events, [Kai Tak Sports Park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kai_Tak_Sports_Park "Kai Tak Sports Park") anchors the city's newest entertainment hub, built on the former airport site, with a 50,000-seat main stadium and a retractable roof. [Hong Kong Stadium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Stadium "Hong Kong Stadium") remains a key venue for football and major fixtures in Causeway Bay, with a capacity of 40,116. [AsiaWorld-Expo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AsiaWorld-Expo "AsiaWorld-Expo") near the airport hosts concerts, exhibitions, and large-scale conventions across multiple halls and an arena-style space. [Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Convention_and_Exhibition_Centre "Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre") is the core waterfront venue in Wan Chai for exhibitions, meetings, and shows.

## Spectator sports

### Badminton

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/HK_%E8%AA%BF%E6%99%AF%E5%B6%BA%E9%AB%94%E8%82%B2%E9%A4%A8_Tiu_Keng_Leng_Sports_Centre_Badminton_courts_Jan-2018_IX1_06.jpg/250px-HK_%E8%AA%BF%E6%99%AF%E5%B6%BA%E9%AB%94%E8%82%B2%E9%A4%A8_Tiu_Keng_Leng_Sports_Centre_Badminton_courts_Jan-2018_IX1_06.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:HK_調景嶺體育館_Tiu_Keng_Leng_Sports_Centre_Badminton_courts_Jan-2018_IX1_06.jpg)

Tiu Keng Leng Sports Centre Badminton courts

Badminton stands as one of the most popular and historically significant sports in Hong Kong,[[356]](#cite_note-Sport_in_Hong_Kong-363) governed by the Badminton Association of Hong Kong, China since its establishment in 1934. The sport is designated as a Tier A elite discipline at the [Hong Kong Sports Institute](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Sports_Institute "Hong Kong Sports Institute"), which provides world-class training and scientific support to the national squad. This institutional backing has allowed the city to consistently produce top-tier talent capable of competing at the [BWF World Tour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BWF_World_Tour "BWF World Tour") and the [Olympic Games](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games").

The current professional landscape is highlighted by the [mixed doubles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mixed_doubles "Mixed doubles") pairing of [Tang Chun-man](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tang_Chun_Man "Tang Chun Man") and [Tse Ying-suet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tse_Ying_Suet "Tse Ying Suet"), who achieved a career-high world ranking of number two. The duo remains a dominant force on the international circuit, recently securing a historic gold medal at the 2025 [Badminton Asia Championships](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Badminton_Asia_Championships "Badminton Asia Championships") and a podium finish at the 2026 [All England Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/All_England_Open_Badminton_Championships "All England Open Badminton Championships"). In the men's singles discipline, [Ng Ka-long Angus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ng_Ka_Long "Ng Ka Long") has been a mainstay of the global top ten, reaching a peak ranking of number six. He is supported by teammate [Lee Cheuk-yiu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lee_Cheuk_Yiu "Lee Cheuk Yiu"), a former [Hong Kong Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Open_(badminton) "Hong Kong Open (badminton)") champion who has maintained a strong international presence with a career-high ranking of thirteen. On the local front, the sport enjoys immense participation through a robust network of community clubs and school programs. The annual [Hong Kong Open](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Open_(badminton) "Hong Kong Open (badminton)"), a [BWF World Tour Super 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BWF_World_Tour "BWF World Tour") event held at the [Hong Kong Coliseum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Coliseum "Hong Kong Coliseum").

### Basketball

See also: [Hong Kong men's national basketball team](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_men's_national_basketball_team "Hong Kong men's national basketball team")

Basketball is one of the most popular sports in Hong Kong,[[356]](#cite_note-Sport_in_Hong_Kong-363) with the [A1 Division](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_A1_Division_Championship "Hong Kong A1 Division Championship") Championship serving as the city's top-tier amateur league since 1954. Historically, the sport gained regional prominence through the success of clubs like Regal, which won the 1997 ABC Champions Cup, and more recently, [Hong Kong Eastern](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Eastern "Hong Kong Eastern").

### Cricket

Main article: [Cricket in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cricket_in_Hong_Kong "Cricket in Hong Kong")

Cricket in Hong Kong has been played since at least 1841. Like most cricketing nations, it was part of the British Empire. The national cricket team has been active since 1866, and the [Hong Kong Cricket Association](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Cricket_Association "Hong Kong Cricket Association") was granted associate membership of the [International Cricket Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC) in 1969. Hong Kong hosted the [Hong Kong Cricket Sixes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Cricket_Sixes "Hong Kong Cricket Sixes"), an ICC sanctioned event that features teams of six players in a six over competition annually till 2012. The [Hong Kong Cricket Team](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Cricket_Team "Hong Kong Cricket Team") qualified for [2014 ICC World Twenty20](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2014_ICC_World_Twenty20 "2014 ICC World Twenty20") and caused a major upset by defeating hosts Bangladesh.

### Fencing

The sport reached its peak during the 2020 and 2024 [Olympic Games](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"). [Cheung Ka-long](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cheung_Ka-long "Cheung Ka-long") became a national icon by winning back-to-back gold medals in men's individual foil, while [Vivian Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vivian_Kong "Vivian Kong") secured gold in women's individual [épée](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Épée "Épée") in [Paris 2024](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2024_Summer_Olympics "2024 Summer Olympics").[[357]](#cite_note-364)[[358]](#cite_note-365)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/HK_Asian_Cup_group_photo.jpg/250px-HK_Asian_Cup_group_photo.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:HK_Asian_Cup_group_photo.jpg)

[Hong Kong National Football Team](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_national_football_team "Hong Kong national football team")

### Football

Main article: [Football in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Football_in_Hong_Kong "Football in Hong Kong")

Football is one of the most popular sports in Hong Kong, with the [Hong Kong Premier League](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Premier_League "Hong Kong Premier League") being the only fully professional sports league in the city.[[356]](#cite_note-Sport_in_Hong_Kong-363)[[359]](#cite_note-366)[[360]](#cite_note-367)[[361]](#cite_note-368)

### Horse racing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/HKIR_20231210_Hong_Kong_Cup_%28racing%29.jpg/330px-HKIR_20231210_Hong_Kong_Cup_%28racing%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:HKIR_20231210_Hong_Kong_Cup_(racing).jpg)

[Longines Hong Kong International Races](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_Races "Hong Kong International Races")

Horse racing is by far the most popular spectator sport in Hong Kong and generates the largest [horse race gambling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Betting_on_horse_racing "Betting on horse racing") turnover in the world. [British tradition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Horseracing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Horseracing in the United Kingdom") left its mark as one of the most important entertainment and gambling institutions in Hong Kong. Established as the Royal [Hong Kong Jockey Club](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Jockey_Club "Hong Kong Jockey Club") in 1884, the non-profit organization conducts nearly 700 races every season at the two race tracks in [Happy Valley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Happy_Valley_Racecourse "Happy Valley Racecourse") and [Sha Tin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sha_Tin_Racecourse "Sha Tin Racecourse"). The sport annually draws in over 11% of Hong Kong's tax revenue. Off-track betting is available from overseas bookmakers. In 2009, betting on horse races generated an average US$12.7 million in gambling turnover per race, 6 times larger than its closest rival [France](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/France "France") at US$2 million while the [United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States "United States") only generated $250,000. Some consider betting on horse races an investment.

### Rugby union

Main article: [Rugby union in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Hong_Kong "Rugby union in Hong Kong")

The [Hong Kong Rugby Football Union](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Rugby_Football_Union "Hong Kong Rugby Football Union") was founded in 1953. Rugby is long established, partly as a result of its being a [British colony](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong").

[Hong Kong Sevens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Sevens "Hong Kong Sevens") is held annually in Hong Kong and is considered the premier tournament on the [World Rugby Sevens Series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Rugby_Sevens_Series "World Rugby Sevens Series") competition.

[Hong Kong national rugby union team](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_national_rugby_union_team "Hong Kong national rugby union team") is ranked 23rd in the world with a peak rating of 21st (2018–2022).Hong Kong is one of the best teams in Asia and has finished first place in the [Asia Rugby Championship](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asia_Rugby_Championship "Asia Rugby Championship") in 2018, 2019, 2022, 2023, 2024 and 2025. They qualified for the [2027 Rugby World Cup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2027_Men's_Rugby_World_Cup "2027 Men's Rugby World Cup") for the first time by winning the [2025 Asia Rugby Championship](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025_Asia_Rugby_Championship "2025 Asia Rugby Championship").

The [Hong Kong national rugby sevens team](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_national_rugby_sevens_team "Hong Kong national rugby sevens team") is widely regarded as the best [rugby sevens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens") team in Asia and regular participant in the [Rugby World Cup Sevens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup_Sevens "Rugby World Cup Sevens"). Hong Kong has won the [Asian Sevens Series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asian_Sevens_Series "Asian Sevens Series") a record 8 times and the [Asian Games](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games") 2 times.

## Education

Main article: [Education in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Education_in_Hong_Kong "Education in Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Bishop%27s_House%2C_Anglican_Church%2C_Hong_Kong.JPG/250px-Bishop%27s_House%2C_Anglican_Church%2C_Hong_Kong.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Bishop's_House,_Anglican_Church,_Hong_Kong.JPG)

Former campus of [St. Paul's College](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St._Paul's_College,_Hong_Kong "St. Paul's College, Hong Kong"), the first school established in the colonial era

Education in Hong Kong is managed primarily by the [Hong Kong Education Bureau](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Education_Bureau "Hong Kong Education Bureau") and is largely modelled on [that of the United Kingdom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Education_in_the_United_Kingdom "Education in the United Kingdom"), particularly the [English system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Education_in_England "Education in England").[[362]](#cite_note-369) Children are required to attend school from age 6 until the completion of secondary education, generally at age 18.[[363]](#cite_note-nss-370)[[364]](#cite_note-HKEduLi-371) At the end of secondary schooling, local school students take a public examination and are awarded the [Hong Kong Diploma of Secondary Education](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Diploma_of_Secondary_Education "Hong Kong Diploma of Secondary Education") upon successful completion of the exam.[[365]](#cite_note-372)

Of residents aged 15 and older, 81% completed lower-secondary education, 66% graduated from an upper secondary school, 32% attended a non-degree tertiary program, and 24% earned a bachelor's degree or higher.[[366]](#cite_note-373)

Mandatory education has contributed to an adult literacy rate of 95.7%.[[367]](#cite_note-374) This literacy rate is lower than that of other developed economies because of the influx of refugees from mainland China during the post-war colonial era; much of the elderly population were not formally educated because of war and poverty.[[368]](#cite_note-375)[[369]](#cite_note-376)

Comprehensive schools fall into three categories: government-run public schools; subsidised schools, including government-aided and grant schools; and private schools, often run by religious organisations, that base admissions on academic merit. These schools are subject to the Education Bureau's curriculum guidelines. Private schools subsidised under the [Direct Subsidy Scheme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Direct_Subsidy_Scheme "Direct Subsidy Scheme"), and international schools fall outside of this system and may elect to use differing curricula and teach using other languages.[[364]](#cite_note-HKEduLi-371)

Hong Kong students perform exceptionally well in global benchmarks. In [PISA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") 2022 (latest available results), Hong Kong ranked in the top tier worldwide; 4th in [mathematics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics") and top 10 in [reading](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reading "Reading") and [science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science "Science"). This places it well above the [OECD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OECD "OECD") average and among the highest-performing systems globally.[[370]](#cite_note-377)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/St._Paul%27s_Co-educational_College_2021.jpg/330px-St._Paul%27s_Co-educational_College_2021.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:St._Paul's_Co-educational_College_2021.jpg)

[St. Paul's Co-educational College](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St._Paul's_Co-educational_College "St. Paul's Co-educational College")

Schools in Hong Kong offer a wide range of curriculums, the [Hong Kong Diploma of Secondary Education](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Diploma_of_Secondary_Education "Hong Kong Diploma of Secondary Education"), [International Baccalaureate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Baccalaureate "International Baccalaureate"), [GCE A-level](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GCE_A-level "GCE A-level"), [IGCSEs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IGCSEs "IGCSEs") and [Advanced Placement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Advanced_Placement "Advanced Placement") are most popular.

Hong Kong Schools offer world-class education with top-tier, high-ranking schools such as [St Paul's Co-educational College](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St_Paul's_Co-educational_College "St Paul's Co-educational College") ranked 2nd in Global Top IB Schools 2025 with a 42.4 average score. While schools such as [Diocesan Boys' School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diocesan_Boys'_School "Diocesan Boys' School") and [German Swiss International School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_Swiss_International_School "German Swiss International School") respectively also rank within the top 10.[[371]](#cite_note-378)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Hong_Kong_International_School.JPG/330px-Hong_Kong_International_School.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_International_School.JPG)

[Hong Kong International School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_School "Hong Kong International School")

[German Swiss International School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_Swiss_International_School "German Swiss International School") 91% of all grades awarded to Year 11 students were A\*/A or 9/8/7 level.

[Hong Kong International School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_School "Hong Kong International School") has exceptional [AP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Advanced_Placement "Advanced Placement") results, In 2025, 80% of students earned scores of 4 or 5. The school follows a US-style curriculum and prepares students for US universities.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Harrow_International_School_Hong_Kong_202201.png/330px-Harrow_International_School_Hong_Kong_202201.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Harrow_International_School_Hong_Kong_202201.png)

[Harrow International School Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harrow_International_School_Hong_Kong "Harrow International School Hong Kong")

There are also many overseas British independent schools in Hong Kong such as [Harrow International School Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harrow_International_School_Hong_Kong "Harrow International School Hong Kong") where 90% of students received (A\*-B), Wycombe Abbey School Hong Kong, [Malvern College Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malvern_College_Hong_Kong "Malvern College Hong Kong") and Shrewsbury International School Hong Kong.

Moreover, there are many schools that cover various regions of the world such as; [Australian International School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Australian_International_School_Hong_Kong "Australian International School Hong Kong"), American International School, [Canadian International School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canadian_International_School_of_Hong_Kong "Canadian International School of Hong Kong"), [French International School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/French_International_School_of_Hong_Kong "French International School of Hong Kong"), Korean International School among others.

Local schools in Hong Kong are categorized into three "bands" based on academic performance, with Band 1 schools being the most prestigious and competitive. These institutions are often criticized for being high-pressure environments. Band 1 schools include [Diocesan Boys' School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diocesan_Boys'_School "Diocesan Boys' School"), [Diocesan Girls' School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diocesan_Girls'_School "Diocesan Girls' School"), [Ying Wa College](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ying_Wa_College "Ying Wa College"), [St. Paul's College](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St._Paul's_College,_Hong_Kong "St. Paul's College, Hong Kong") and [La Salle College](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/La_Salle_College "La Salle College").

### Medium of instruction

At primary and secondary school levels, the government maintains a policy of "mother tongue instruction"; most schools use Cantonese as the [medium of instruction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medium_of_instruction "Medium of instruction"), with written education in both Chinese and English. Other languages being used as the medium of instruction in non-international school education include [English](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/English_language "English language") and [Putonghua](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") ([Mandarin Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese")). Secondary schools emphasise "[bi-literacy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biliteracy "Biliteracy") and [tri-lingualism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trilingualism "Trilingualism")", which has encouraged the proliferation of spoken Mandarin language education.[[372]](#cite_note-379)

English is the official medium of instruction and assessment for most university programmes in Hong Kong, although Cantonese is predominant in informal discussions among local students and professors.[[373]](#cite_note-380)[[374]](#cite_note-381)[[375]](#cite_note-382)[[376]](#cite_note-383)[[377]](#cite_note-384)[[378]](#cite_note-385)

### Tertiary education

Main article: [Higher education in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Higher_education_in_Hong_Kong "Higher education in Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Main_Building_HKU_20100926_03.JPG/330px-Main_Building_HKU_20100926_03.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Main_Building_HKU_20100926_03.JPG)

[University of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Hong_Kong "University of Hong Kong") main building

Hong Kong has twelve universities.
