The [University of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Hong_Kong "University of Hong Kong") (HKU) ranked [QS Asia#1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings") was founded as the city's first institute of higher education in 1911 during the early colonial period.[[379]](#cite_note-386) The [Chinese University of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_University_of_Hong_Kong "Chinese University of Hong Kong") (CUHK) was established in 1963 to fill the need for a university that taught using Chinese as its primary language of instruction.[[380]](#cite_note-387) Along with the [Hong Kong University of Science and Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Hong Kong University of Science and Technology") (HKUST) established in 1991, these universities are consistently ranked among the top 20 or top 50 universities worldwide.[[381]](#cite_note-:1-388)[[382]](#cite_note-:2-389)[[383]](#cite_note-:3-390)

The [Hong Kong Polytechnic University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Polytechnic_University "Hong Kong Polytechnic University") (PolyU)[[384]](#cite_note-391) and [City University of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/City_University_of_Hong_Kong "City University of Hong Kong") (CityU), both granted university status in 1994, are consistently ranked among the top 50 or top 100 universities worldwide.[[381]](#cite_note-:1-388)[[382]](#cite_note-:2-389)[[383]](#cite_note-:3-390) The [Hong Kong Baptist University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Baptist_University "Hong Kong Baptist University") (HKBU) was granted university status in 1994[[385]](#cite_note-392) and is a liberal arts institution. [Hong Kong Metropolitan University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Metropolitan_University "Hong Kong Metropolitan University") (formerly as Open University of Hong Kong before 2021) (attaining status in 1997),[[386]](#cite_note-393) [Lingnan University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lingnan_University "Lingnan University") (in 1999),[[387]](#cite_note-394) [Hong Kong Shue Yan University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Shue_Yan_University "Hong Kong Shue Yan University") (in 2006),[[388]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELegCo_Grant_to_Shue_Yan_University2007-395) [Education University of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Education_University_of_Hong_Kong "Education University of Hong Kong") (in 2016), [Hang Seng University of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hang_Seng_University_of_Hong_Kong "Hang Seng University of Hong Kong") (in 2018) and [Saint Francis University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saint_Francis_University_(Hong_Kong) "Saint Francis University (Hong Kong)") (in 2024)[[389]](#cite_note-396) all attained full university status in subsequent years.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/The_University_of_Chicago_Francis_and_Rose_Yuen_Campus.jpg/330px-The_University_of_Chicago_Francis_and_Rose_Yuen_Campus.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:The_University_of_Chicago_Francis_and_Rose_Yuen_Campus.jpg)

[University of Chicago Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Hong_Kong "University of Chicago Hong Kong")

Overseas universities in Hong Kong often operate as branch campuses or partnerships offering accredited degrees, such as the [University of Chicago Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Hong_Kong "University of Chicago Hong Kong"), [University of Sunderland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Sunderland "University of Sunderland"), [University of Wollongong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Wollongong "University of Wollongong") in Hong Kong. These institutions, provide local and internationally recognized qualifications.

In 2026, [QS Best Student Cities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings") ranked Hong Kong as the 17th best city for university students. 7th in best Asia for best student cities. Noting, high scores in employer activity, desirability, and diverse student population.

**Subject Rankings**

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Hong_Kong_Academy_for_Performing_Arts_Expansion_2019.jpg/330px-Hong_Kong_Academy_for_Performing_Arts_Expansion_2019.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_Academy_for_Performing_Arts_Expansion_2019.jpg)

[Hong Kong Academy for Performing Arts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Academy_for_Performing_Arts "Hong Kong Academy for Performing Arts")

In the 2026 [QS World University Rankings by Subject](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings"), Hong Kong's higher education sector demonstrated significant global competitiveness, with five academic programs placing within the world's top 10. The [University of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Hong_Kong "University of Hong Kong") (HKU) maintained its status as the city's most decorated institution, securing global 2nd and 5th positions for Dentistry and Education, respectively. Other top-tier performances included the [Chinese University of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CUHK_Faculty_of_Medicine "CUHK Faculty of Medicine") (CUHK) ranking 6th globally for Nursing, the [Education University of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Education_University_of_Hong_Kong "Education University of Hong Kong") (EdUHK) placing 7th in Education, and the [Hong Kong Academy for Performing Arts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Academy_for_Performing_Arts "Hong Kong Academy for Performing Arts") (HKAPA) breaking into the top 10 for Performing Arts. The highest-ranked performing arts institution in Asia.

## Healthcare

Main article: [Health in Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Health_in_Hong_Kong "Health in Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/HK_HongKongSanatoriumAndHospital_Overview.JPG/250px-HK_HongKongSanatoriumAndHospital_Overview.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:HK_HongKongSanatoriumAndHospital_Overview.JPG)

[Hong Kong Sanatorium and Hospital](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Sanatorium_&_Hospital "Hong Kong Sanatorium & Hospital") ([7th Tallest Hospital in the world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_tallest_hospitals "List of tallest hospitals"))

Hong Kong is one of the healthiest places in the world. Due to early health education, professional health services, and well-developed healthcare and medication system.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Queen_Mary_Hospital_25-11-2023.jpg/250px-Queen_Mary_Hospital_25-11-2023.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Queen_Mary_Hospital_25-11-2023.jpg)

[Queen Mary Hospital](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Queen_Mary_Hospital_(Hong_Kong) "Queen Mary Hospital (Hong Kong)")

Public healthcare in Hong Kong is recognized for its high-quality medical outcomes. The territory has [highest life expectancy in the world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_life_expectancy "List of countries by life expectancy") at 85.9 years. Hong Kong has one of the [lowest infant mortality rates in the world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_infant_mortality_rate "List of countries by infant mortality rate"), with recent estimates placing it around 1.7 to 2.5 deaths per 1,000 live births. However the public system also faces challenges, including high patient volumes, overcrowding in emergency departments, and long wait times for specialist appointments and elective procedures.

Healthcare in Hong Kong is provided through two categories consisting of [public](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_hospital "Public hospital") and [private hospitals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Private_hospital "Private hospital"). While private hospitals services cater to patients seeking shorter wait times and personalized care, the majority of the population relies on the extensive public healthcare system. Public hospitals and clinics are heavily subsidized by the government, ensuring affordable access to a wide range of medical services.

Hong Kong's healthcare system is managed primarily by the [Hospital Authority](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hospital_Authority "Hospital Authority"), which was established in 1990 to oversee and operate all public hospitals in the region. The system is organized into seven geographic clusters that group hospitals and associated healthcare facilities based on their distribution across the territory. As of 2026, Hong Kong has a total of 42 [public hospitals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_hospitals "Public hospitals") and 11 [private hospitals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Private_hospitals "Private hospitals").

The [University of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Hong_Kong "University of Hong Kong") (HKU) [Li Ka Shing Faculty of Medicine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Li_Ka_Shing_Faculty_of_Medicine "Li Ka Shing Faculty of Medicine") is consistently ranked among the top [medical schools](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medical_school "Medical school") globally and is a leader in Asia, ranking 18th worldwide in the [Times Higher Education](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings") 2026 Rankings for [Clinical and Health](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Health_care "Health care") and 24th in the [QS World University Rankings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings") [Medicine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medicine "Medicine").

## Media

Main article: [Media of Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Media_of_Hong_Kong "Media of Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/HK_Newspaper_%E6%98%8E%E5%A0%B1_Ming_Pao_%E9%A6%99%E6%B8%AF%E7%B6%93%E6%BF%9F%E6%97%A5%E5%A0%B1_HKET_%E5%8D%97%E8%8F%AF%E6%97%A9%E5%A0%B1_South_China_Morning_Post_SCMP_%E6%9D%B1%E6%96%B9%E6%97%A5%E5%A0%B1_Oriental_Daily_News_%E9%87%91%E8%9E%8D%E6%99%82%E5%A0%B1_Financial_Times_%E4%BF%A1%E5%A0%B1%E8%B2%A1%E7%B6%93%E6%96%B0%E8%81%9E_Economic_Journal_HKEJ_Nov_2017_IX1_HKICPA_library_reading.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:HK_Newspaper_明報_Ming_Pao_香港經濟日報_HKET_南華早報_South_China_Morning_Post_SCMP_東方日報_Oriental_Daily_News_金融時報_Financial_Times_信報財經新聞_Economic_Journal_HKEJ_Nov_2017_IX1_HKICPA_library_reading.jpg)

Hong Kong Newspapers

 [![Modern, green-and-white building with dish antennas on top](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/TVBuddy%2C_TVB_City_20210521.jpg/250px-TVBuddy%2C_TVB_City_20210521.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:TVBuddy,_TVB_City_20210521.jpg)

[TVB City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TVB_City "TVB City"), headquarters of Hong Kong's first over-the-air television station

Most newspapers in Hong Kong are written in Chinese. There are also English-language newspapers, the major one being the *[South China Morning Post](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_China_Morning_Post "South China Morning Post")*, with *[The Standard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Standard_(Hong_Kong) "The Standard (Hong Kong)")* serving as a business-oriented alternative. A variety of Chinese-language newspapers are published daily; the most prominent are *[Ming Pao](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ming_Pao "Ming Pao")* and *[Oriental Daily News](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oriental_Daily_News "Oriental Daily News")*. Local publications are often partisan, with pro-Beijing or pro-democracy sympathies. The central government has a print-media presence in the territory through the state-owned *[Ta Kung Pao](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ta_Kung_Pao "Ta Kung Pao")* and *[Wen Wei Po](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wen_Wei_Po "Wen Wei Po")*.[[390]](#cite_note-397) Several international publications have regional operations in Hong Kong, including *[The Wall Street Journal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")*, the *[Financial Times](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")*, *[USA Today](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")*, [TimeOut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time_Out_(magazine) "Time Out (magazine)"), *[The Nikkei](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Nikkei "The Nikkei") and [Vogue Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vogue_Hong_Kong "Vogue Hong Kong").*[[391]](#cite_note-398)

Four [free-to-air](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Free-to-air "Free-to-air") television broadcasters operate in the territory; [TVB](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TVB "TVB"), [HKTVE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HK_Television_Entertainment "HK Television Entertainment"), [HOY](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/I-CABLE_HOY_Limited "I-CABLE HOY Limited") and [Phoenix Television](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phoenix_Television "Phoenix Television") air eight [digital](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_television "Digital television") channels.[[392]](#cite_note-broadcasterList-399)[[393]](#cite_note-400) TVB, Hong Kong's dominant television network, has an 80% viewer share.[[394]](#cite_note-401) [Paid television services](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Television_in_Hong_Kong "Television in Hong Kong") operated by [PCCW](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PCCW "PCCW") offer hundreds of additional channels and cater to a variety of audiences.[[395]](#cite_note-402) [RTHK](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RTHK "RTHK") is the public broadcaster, providing seven radio channels and six free-to-air television channels.[[396]](#cite_note-403)[[397]](#cite_note-404)[[398]](#cite_note-405) Ten non-domestic broadcasters air programming for the territory's foreign population.[[392]](#cite_note-broadcasterList-399) Access to media and information over the Internet is not subject to mainland Chinese regulations, including the [Great Firewall](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Firewall "Great Firewall"), but [local internet censorship](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Internet_censorship_in_Hong_Kong "Internet censorship in Hong Kong") applies.[[399]](#cite_note-406)

# hong

## [noun](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/noun)

[ˈhäŋ](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/hong?pronunciation&lang=en_us&dir=h&file=hong0001 "How to pronounce hong (audio)")

ˈhȯŋ

**:**a commercial establishment or house of foreign trade in China

## Word History

Etymology

Chinese (Guangdong) _hòhng_, literally, row

First Known Use

1726, in the meaning defined [above](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/hong#h1)

Time Traveler

The first known use of _hong_ was in 1726

[See more words from the same year](https://www.merriam-webster.com/time-traveler/1726?src=defrecirc-timetraveler-etycard)
